decision.title: Government Awaits New Airport Valuation as Concession Process Advances Toward Parliamentary Review
decision.summary: The government is awaiting a fresh asset valuation of Airports of Montenegro before the concession proposal can proceed to a cabinet session and potentially to Parliament, which must approve the deal if the valuation exceeds €150 million (the 2018 estimate was €140 million). Three bidders have until May 9 to submit offers in the tender launched in December 2024. The concession of Podgorica and Tivat airports for 25-30 years remains politically contentious, with opposition calling it a sell-off of national assets. Reported by Investitor, aktuelno.me, and Bankar.me.
decision.title_mne: Vlada čeka novu procjenu vrijednosti aerodroma dok proces koncesije napreduje ka parlamentarnom razmatranju
decision.summary_mne: Vlada čeka novu procjenu vrijednosti imovine Aerodroma Crne Gore prije nego što prijedlog koncesije može biti upućen na sjednicu Kabineta i potencijalno pred Skupštinu, koja mora odobriti ugovor ukoliko procjena premaši 150 miliona eura (procjena iz 2018. godine iznosila je 140 miliona eura). Tri ponuđača imaju rok do 9. maja da dostave ponude u tenderu koji je pokrenut u decembru 2024. godine. Koncesija podgoričkog i tivtskog aerodroma na period od 25 do 30 godina ostaje politički osjetljiva tema, a opozicija je opisuje kao rasprodaju nacionalne imovine. Izvještavaju Investitor, aktuelno.me i Bankar.me.

assessments.summary: The government's decision to commission a fresh asset valuation before advancing the airport concession is fiscally prudent and legally necessary. Given that the 2018 valuation of EUR 140 million is almost certainly outdated -- with credible estimates now placing the value at EUR 300-400 million -- the new valuation will trigger the parliamentary approval threshold (EUR 150 million under state asset disposal law), ensuring democratic oversight of Montenegro's largest-ever PPP deal. The concession's potential to deliver EUR 100 million upfront, over EUR 200 million in capital investment, and annual revenue-sharing fees of at least 10% of airport revenue represents a significant fiscal opportunity for a country running a 4% budget deficit and carrying public debt near 60% of GDP. However, the Ministry of Finance has concerns about valuation independence, the adequacy of the revenue-sharing rate, and whether the concession structure maximizes long-term fiscal returns rather than short-term cash.
assessments.executive_summary: The decision to seek a fresh airport valuation is fiscally sound and will correctly trigger parliamentary oversight for what could be Montenegro's largest PPP deal. The main concern is ensuring the valuation is truly independent and that the concession fee structure -- particularly the 10% annual revenue share and EUR 100 million upfront payment -- reflects the airports' true long-term value (potentially EUR 300-400 million), rather than underpricing a strategic national asset during a period of rising tourism revenues.
assessments.reasoning:
From a fiscal policy standpoint, the decision to await a new valuation before proceeding is both legally required and financially sound. The 2018 valuation of EUR 140 million was conducted in a pre-pandemic environment and does not reflect several years of strong tourism recovery, infrastructure improvements, and general asset appreciation across the Western Balkans. Multiple sources, including airport trade union representatives, suggest the current value could range between EUR 300-400 million -- more than double the previous estimate. If the new valuation exceeds EUR 150 million, the Law on State Property mandates that the deal must go before Parliament, which is an important fiscal safeguard for a transaction of this magnitude.

Regarding budget impact, the concession structure as currently reported would deliver: (1) an upfront payment of EUR 100 million, (2) annual concession fees of at least 10% of total airport revenue, and (3) over EUR 200 million in private capital investment over the concession period. For context, Montenegro's annual budget is approximately EUR 2 billion and the country is running a fiscal deficit of around 4% of GDP. An upfront payment of EUR 100 million would represent roughly 5% of total annual budget revenue -- a significant but one-time injection. The annual revenue-sharing fee, assuming airport revenues of approximately EUR 40-50 million per year (growing with tourism), would yield EUR 4-5 million annually, rising over time.

On public debt, Montenegro's debt-to-GDP ratio stands at approximately 58-60% and is projected to rise toward 65% by the end of the decade. While the upfront concession payment could temporarily improve the fiscal position, the Ministry of Finance must ensure that these proceeds are earmarked for debt reduction or strategic capital investment rather than consumed by current expenditure. Reports indicate that 75% of concession revenues may be directed toward municipal strategic projects, which could limit the debt-reduction benefit.

From an EU accession perspective, Montenegro has recently closed Chapter 32 (Financial Control) and is aiming to close all remaining negotiating chapters by end of 2026. A well-structured, transparent concession process would demonstrate adherence to EU public procurement standards, state aid rules, and PPP best practices. However, allegations of process irregularities -- including union concerns about valuation independence and suggestions that the process favours the Korean consortium led by Incheon International Airport Corporation -- could undermine the credibility of the process in the eyes of EU institutions. The European Commission's progress reports consistently flag public procurement transparency as an area requiring improvement.

Concerning foreign investment, the airport concession would be Montenegro's largest-ever PPP deal and a major signal to international investors. Incheon International Airport Corporation, which operates one of the world's top-rated airports, brings significant operational expertise. However, the Ministry notes that the competitive tension in the tender appears limited -- earlier stages reportedly involved only two serious bidders, and the current three-bidder structure needs to be verified as genuinely competitive. Limited competition could suppress the upfront fee and revenue-sharing terms.

The valuation process itself presents risks. The trade union of Airports of Montenegro has publicly called for the valuation to be conducted by multiple independent assessors rather than solely through the Directorate for State Property. This is a reasonable request from a fiscal governance standpoint. A single-source valuation for an asset of this magnitude creates risk of undervaluation, which would directly reduce state revenue. International best practice for airport privatisations (as seen in Greece with Fraport, or in Brazil's airport concession rounds) typically involves multiple independent valuations, including by internationally recognised firms.

Finally, the 25-30 year concession period is a significant commitment. Montenegro's tourism sector is growing rapidly and the airports are monopoly infrastructure assets with captive demand. Locking in a 10% revenue share for three decades may prove insufficient if airport revenues grow substantially -- as they likely will with EU accession, improved connectivity, and expanding Adriatic tourism. A graduated or inflation-indexed revenue-sharing mechanism would better protect the state's long-term fiscal interests.
key_concerns: The 2018 valuation of EUR 140 million is severely outdated; if the new valuation is not conducted with full independence, the state risks underpricing a strategic asset potentially worth EUR 300-400 million

A flat 10% annual revenue share over 25-30 years may significantly undervalue the concession as airport revenues grow with tourism expansion and EU accession, locking Montenegro into below-market returns for decades
The upfront EUR 100 million payment, while substantial, must be ring-fenced for debt reduction or capital investment -- if absorbed into current expenditure it provides no lasting fiscal benefit
Limited competitive tension in the tender (reports of only 2-3 serious bidders) may suppress both the upfront fee and ongoing revenue-sharing terms below what a more competitive process would yield

Trade union and opposition concerns about valuation transparency could create political and legal risks that delay or derail the concession, increasing uncertainty for bidders and potentially reducing offer quality
The allocation of 75% of concession revenues to municipal strategic projects reduces the central government's fiscal flexibility and debt-management capacity
A 25-30 year concession period for monopoly airport infrastructure is unusually long by EU standards and limits the state's ability to renegotiate terms as conditions change
recommendations: Mandate that the new valuation be conducted by at least two independent internationally recognised firms (e.g., from the Big Four or specialised aviation valuers), in addition to the Directorate for State Property assessment, to ensure credibility and accuracy

Restructure the revenue-sharing mechanism from a flat 10% to a graduated scale (e.g., 10% up to EUR 50M revenue, 15% for EUR 50-80M, 20% above EUR 80M) to capture upside as airport traffic grows over the 25-30 year period
Ring-fence the EUR 100 million upfront payment in a sovereign wealth or debt-reduction fund, legally prohibiting its use for current budgetary expenditure
Reduce the concession period to 20-25 years maximum, in line with EU PPP best practices, with a mid-term review clause at year 12-15 allowing renegotiation of financial terms
Include a minimum annual concession fee (floor) indexed to inflation to protect state revenues in the event of temporary traffic downturns

Ensure full parliamentary debate with access to all financial models, independent valuations, and IFC advisory reports before any vote, aligning with Chapter 32 commitments on financial control transparency
Require the concessionaire to maintain a minimum level of domestic employment and local procurement, protecting the fiscal base (income tax and social contributions) from the airport workforce

counter_proposal.title: Fiscally Optimised Airport Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Finance would restructure the concession to maximise long-term state revenue through a graduated revenue-sharing model, shorter concession period, mandatory independent dual valuation, and strict ring-fencing of upfront proceeds for debt reduction. Rather than accepting a flat 10% revenue share for 30 years, we would implement a progressive fee structure that captures growing airport revenues, combined with inflation-indexed minimum payments and a mid-term renegotiation clause.
key_changes: Require dual independent international valuation (two separate firms) before any cabinet or parliamentary vote
Implement graduated revenue sharing: 10% baseline rising to 20% as airport revenues exceed defined thresholds
Shorten concession to 22 years with a mandatory financial review at year 12
Legislatively ring-fence the upfront EUR 100M+ payment for public debt reduction only
Add inflation-indexed minimum annual fee floor (starting at EUR 3 million, adjusted annually by HICP)
Require full disclosure of IFC financial models and advisory reports to Parliament and public
expected_benefits: Higher long-term state revenue capture estimated at EUR 30-50 million additional over 22 years through graduated sharing
Direct debt-to-GDP improvement of approximately 1.5 percentage points from ring-fenced upfront payment applied to public debt
Greater valuation credibility reducing political and legal risk of post-award challenges
Stronger EU accession signalling through transparent PPP governance aligned with Chapter 32 standards
Preserved state flexibility through shorter concession period and mid-term review clause
Protected revenue floor during economic downturns through inflation-indexed minimum payments
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Graduated revenue-sharing models are standard in international airport concessions (used in Brazil, India, and Greece). Dual independent valuations are routine for transactions above EUR 100 million. Shortening the concession by 5-8 years may modestly reduce the upfront fee by EUR 10-15 million but would be more than offset by higher cumulative revenue-sharing payments. The main political risk is that these changes could delay the process by 3-6 months, but this is acceptable given the 25-30 year commitment being made.
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo finansija bi restrukturiralo koncesiju kako bi se maksimizovao dugoročni državni prihod kroz model postepene podjele prihoda, kraći period koncesije, obaveznu nezavisnu dvostruku procjenu vrijednosti i strogo obezbjeđivanje unaprijed plaćenih sredstava za smanjenje duga. Umjesto prihvatanja fiksnog udjela od 10% prihoda na 30 godina, implementirali bismo progresivnu strukturu naknada koja prati rast aerodromskih prihoda, u kombinaciji sa plaćanjima minimuma indeksiranim prema inflaciji i klauzulom o pregovaranju na sredini perioda.
key_changes_mne: Zahtijevati dvostruku nezavisnu međunarodnu procjenu vrijednosti (dvije odvojene firme) prije bilo kakvog glasanja u kabinetu ili parlamentu
Uvesti postepenu podjelu prihoda: osnova od 10% koja raste do 20% kada aerodromski prihodi pređu definisane pragove
Skratiti koncesiju na 22 godine uz obaveznu finansijsku reviziju u 12. godini
Zakonodavno zaštititi unaprijed plaćeni iznos od 100+ miliona EUR isključivo za smanjenje javnog duga
Dodati pod za minimalnu godišnju naknadu indeksiranu prema inflaciji (počev od 3 miliona EUR, usklađivano godišnje prema HICP-u)
Zahtijevati potpuno obelodanjivanje finansijskih modela i savjetodavnih izvještaja IFC-a Parlamentu i javnosti
expected_benefits_mne: Veći dugoročni državni prihod – procijenjenih 30–50 miliona EUR dodatno tokom 22 godine kroz postepenu podjelu
Direktno poboljšanje odnosa duga i BDP-a za otprilike 1,5 procentnih poena zahvaljujući zaštićenoj unaprijed plaćenoj sumi usmjerenoj na javni dug
Veći kredibilitet procjene vrijednosti koji smanjuje politički i pravni rizik od osporavanja nakon dodjele
Jači signal EU integracija kroz transparentno upravljanje JPP-om usklađeno sa standardima Poglavlja 32
Očuvana državna fleksibilnost kroz kraći period koncesije i klauzulu o preispitivanju na sredini perioda
Zaštićen prag prihoda tokom ekonomskih padova kroz plaćanja minimuma indeksirana prema inflaciji
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Modeli postepene podjele prihoda su standardni u međunarodnim aerodromskim koncesijama (primjenjuju se u Brazilu, Indiji i Grčkoj). Dvostruke nezavisne procjene su uobičajene za transakcije iznad 100 miliona EUR. Skraćivanje koncesije za 5–8 godina može skromno smanjiti unaprijed plaćenu naknadu za 10–15 miliona EUR, ali to bi s viškom bilo nadoknađeno višim kumulativnim uplatama iz podjele prihoda. Glavni politički rizik je da bi ove promjene mogle odložiti proces za 3–6 mjeseci, što je prihvatljivo s obzirom na obavezu koja se preuzima na 25–30 godina.

assessments.summary_mne: Odluka Vlade da naruči novu procjenu imovine prije nego što nastavi sa dodjelo koncesije aerodroma fiskalno je oprezna i pravno neophodna. S obzirom na to da je procjena iz 2018. godine u iznosu od 140 miliona EUR gotovo sigurno zastarjela — pri čemu pouzdane procjene sada vrijednost postavljaju na 300–400 miliona EUR — nova procjena će pokrenuti prag za parlamentarno odobrenje (150 miliona EUR prema zakonu o raspolaganju državnom imovinom), čime se osigurava demokratski nadzor nad najvećim PPP poslom u istoriji Crne Gore. Potencijal koncesije da donese 100 miliona EUR unaprijed, više od 200 miliona EUR kapitalnih investicija i godišnje naknade za podjelu prihoda od najmanje 10% prihoda aerodroma predstavlja značajnu fiskalnu priliku za zemlju koja bilježi budžetski deficit od 4% i javni dug blizu 60% BDP-a. Međutim, Ministarstvo finansija ima zabrinutosti u pogledu nezavisnosti procjene, adekvatnosti stope podjele prihoda i toga da li struktura koncesije maksimizuje dugoročne fiskalne prinose umjesto kratkoročnog prihoda od gotovine.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Odluka da se zatraži nova procjena aerodroma fiskalno je opravdana i ispravno će pokrenuti parlamentarni nadzor nad onim što bi mogao biti najveći PPP posao Crne Gore. Ključna briga jeste osigurati da procjena bude istinski nezavisna i da struktura koncesione naknade — posebno godišnji udio u prihodima od 10% i unapredna uplata od 100 miliona EUR — odražava stvarnu dugoročnu vrijednost aerodroma (potencijalno 300–400 miliona EUR), umjesto da se strateško nacionalno sredstvo potcijenuje u periodu rasta prihoda od turizma.
key_concerns_mne: Procjena iz 2018. godine u iznosu od 140 miliona EUR ozbiljno je zastarjela; ukoliko nova procjena ne bude sprovedena uz potpunu nezavisnost, država riskiruje potcjenjivanje strateškog sredstva čija vrijednost potencijalno iznosi 300–400 miliona EUR

Fiksni godišnji udio u prihodima od 10% tokom 25–30 godina može značajno potcijeniti koncesiju kako prihodi aerodroma budu rasli usljed razvoja turizma i pristupanja EU, čime bi se Crna Gora na decenije vezala za prinose ispod tržišnih

Unapredni iznos od 100 miliona EUR, iako značajan, mora biti namjenski rezervisan za smanjenje duga ili kapitalne investicije — ukoliko bude usmjeren u tekuću potrošnju, neće donijeti trajnu fiskalnu korist
Ograničena konkurentska dinamika u tenderu (izvještava se o svega 2–3 ozbiljna ponuđača) može umanjiti i unaprednu naknadu i uslove podjele prihoda ispod nivoa koji bi osigurao konkurentniji postupak

Zabrinutost sindikata i opozicije u pogledu transparentnosti procjene može stvoriti politički i pravni rizik koji bi odložio ili ugrozio koncesiju, povećavajući neizvjesnost za ponuđače i potencijalno smanjujući kvalitet ponuda
Raspodjela 75% prihoda od koncesije za municipalne strateške projekte smanjuje fiskalnu fleksibilnost centralne vlade i njenu sposobnost upravljanja dugom

Period koncesije od 25–30 godina za monopolsku aerodromsku infrastrukturu neuobičajeno je dug prema standardima EU i ograničava sposobnost države da pregovara o uslovima kako se okolnosti budu mijenjale
recommendations_mne: Obavezati da novu procjenu sprovedu najmanje dvije nezavisne međunarodno priznate firme (npr. iz Velike četvorke ili specijalizovane firme za vrednovanje u oblasti avijacije), pored procjene Direkcije za imovinu, kako bi se osigurali vjerodostojnost i tačnost

Restrukturirati mehanizam podjele prihoda sa fiksnih 10% na stepenasto skaliranu šemu (npr. 10% do 50M EUR prihoda, 15% za 50–80M EUR, 20% iznad 80M EUR), kako bi se uhvatilo uvećanje vrijednosti kako saobraćaj u aerodromu bude rastao tokom perioda od 25–30 godina
Namjenski rezervisati unapredni iznos od 100 miliona EUR u fond suvereniteta ili fond za smanjenje duga, uz zakonsku zabranu njegove upotrebe za tekuću budžetsku potrošnju

Skratiti period koncesije na maksimalno 20–25 godina, u skladu sa najboljim praksama EU za JPP, uz klauzulu o reviziji sredinom perioda — u godini 12–15 — koja omogućava pregovaranje o finansijskim uslovima
Uključiti minimalnu godišnju naknadu za koncesiju (najniži prag) indeksiranu prema inflaciji, kako bi se zaštitili državni prihodi u slučaju privremenog pada saobraćaja

Osigurati potpunu parlamentarnu raspravu uz pristup svim finansijskim modelima, nezavisnim procjenama i savjetodavnim izvještajima MMK-a prije glasanja, u skladu s obavezama iz Poglavlja 32 o transparentnosti finansijske kontrole

Obavezati koncesionara da održava minimalni nivo domaćeg zapošljavanja i nabavke od lokalnih dobavljača, čime se štiti fiskalna osnova (porez na dohodak i doprinosi za socijalno osiguranje) radne snage aerodroma

assessments.summary: The airport concession, if structured properly, could attract significant FDI and modernize critical transport infrastructure, but the process has been plagued by delays, legal disputes, lack of social dialogue, and insufficient transparency around asset valuation. With passenger traffic surpassing 3 million in 2025 and airports generating growing revenue, the Ministry of Economy is concerned that the government may be undervaluing a rapidly appreciating strategic asset. The absence of meaningful worker consultation, the unions' unanimous opposition, and the lack of a published comparative analysis (concession vs. state-managed development) raise serious questions about whether this deal maximizes long-term economic value for Montenegro.
assessments.executive_summary: The airport concession process has potential to deliver needed FDI and infrastructure investment, but risks significantly undervaluing a strategic national asset whose traffic has grown 15% year-on-year. The most urgent concerns are the absence of an independent, transparent valuation reflecting current market conditions, and the complete exclusion of workers and unions from the process despite their legal rights to consultation. The Ministry recommends pausing the tender until an independent multi-method valuation is completed and a credible comparative analysis of concession versus state-led development is published.
assessments.reasoning:
FROM AN FDI AND ECONOMIC GROWTH PERSPECTIVE: Airport concessions are a well-established PPP model globally, and attracting experienced international operators (Incheon International Airport Corporation from South Korea, Corporacion America Airports from Luxembourg, and Aeroports de Paris/TAV from France/Turkey) signals genuine investor interest in Montenegro. The IFC's advisory role lends procedural credibility. A successful concession could unlock EUR 200 million or more in investment, improve airport infrastructure, expand route connectivity, and support Montenegro's tourism-dependent economy (tourism contributes roughly 25% of GDP). However, the economic case must be evaluated against the airports' trajectory: passenger traffic reached a record 2.87 million in 2024 and exceeded 3 million in 2025, growth of roughly 15% year-on-year. This growth trajectory suggests the airports are already performing well under state management and their future cash flows are likely substantially higher than historical estimates.

ON ASSET VALUATION: The 2018 valuation of EUR 140 million is clearly outdated. Industry sources and union representatives have suggested the current value may be EUR 300-400 million. The government's decision to commission a new valuation is procedurally correct and legally necessary (the Law on State Property requires Parliamentary approval for disposals exceeding EUR 150 million), but the Ministry of Economy is concerned about the independence and methodology of this valuation. The Sindikat Aerodroma Crne Gore (Airport Workers' Union) has rightly demanded that the valuation be conducted by multiple independent assessors, incorporate both asset-based and income-based (discounted cash flow) methods, and be publicly verifiable. A single government-commissioned valuation by the Administration for State Property (Uprava za drzavnu imovinu) may not meet international best-practice standards for a transaction of this magnitude. The EU Court of Auditors' 2018 Special Report on PPPs in the EU specifically warned against optimism bias and inadequate public sector comparators in PPP deals.

ON LABOR RIGHTS AND SOCIAL DIALOGUE: This is perhaps the most significant concern from the Ministry's perspective. Both representative unions at Airports of Montenegro have unanimously opposed the concession and declared the process irreparably compromised. Under Montenegrin labour law (Zakon o radu), the employer has an obligation to consult with trade unions in cases of collective layoffs (20+ employees within 90 days). More broadly, Montenegro's obligations under EU accession Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) and ILO conventions on freedom of association and collective bargaining require meaningful social dialogue on major restructuring decisions. The unions report being excluded from the tender commission and having no meaningful input into the draft concession agreement's worker protection provisions. The draft agreement reportedly provides for staff transfer to the concessionaire and return to the government on expiry, but details on wage guarantees, collective agreement continuity, retraining programs, and protection against mass layoffs remain unclear or unpublished. This is a serious deficiency. The EU Directive on Transfer of Undertakings (2001/23/EC), which Montenegro must align with as part of the acquis communautaire, requires that employees' rights and obligations under existing employment contracts and collective agreements be preserved during a transfer.

ON PROCESS INTEGRITY: The tender process has been running since 2018-2019 with multiple restarts, legal challenges (including Corporacion America Airports' successful partial objection before the Concessions Commission), and public disputes among government officials. This protracted and contested process erodes investor confidence and increases transaction costs. The opposition's characterization of the deal as a "sell-off" gains traction precisely because of these procedural shortcomings. A transparent, well-justified process would be more resistant to political criticism.

ON COMPETITIVENESS AND SMEs: Airport operations generate significant downstream economic activity for local SMEs, including ground handling, catering, retail, transport, and tourism services. The concession agreement's provisions on non-aeronautical revenue, local procurement requirements, and small business access to airport commercial opportunities will significantly affect Montenegro's SME ecosystem. These terms have not been publicly disclosed in sufficient detail.

ON ENERGY AND SUSTAINABILITY: Modern airport concessions increasingly include environmental and energy efficiency obligations aligned with EU Green Deal targets. It is unclear whether the draft concession agreement includes binding commitments on carbon reduction, renewable energy use, or alignment with Montenegro's Energy Community obligations. Given Montenegro's EU accession trajectory, any 25-30 year agreement should embed these requirements.

COMPARISON WITH ALTERNATIVES: The government has not published a credible public sector comparator or value-for-money analysis comparing the concession model with alternatives such as a state-managed capital investment program (potentially funded by EU pre-accession instruments, IPA III, or international development banks like the EBRD). Given that the airports are profitable and growing, the case for private concession over public investment is not self-evident and needs rigorous demonstration.
key_concerns: The 2018 asset valuation of EUR 140 million is severely outdated given record traffic growth (3M+ passengers in 2025), and the new valuation process lacks confirmed independence and multi-method methodology

Airport workers' unions have been unanimously excluded from meaningful participation in the concession process, violating the spirit of Montenegrin labour law, ILO conventions, and EU accession Chapter 19 requirements on social dialogue
No public sector comparator or value-for-money analysis has been published comparing the concession model against state-managed development funded by EU pre-accession instruments or development banks

The draft concession agreement's worker protection provisions (wage guarantees, collective agreement continuity, anti-layoff safeguards) remain insufficiently detailed or unpublished, creating legal uncertainty under the EU Transfer of Undertakings Directive
The protracted tender process (running since 2018-2019) with multiple legal challenges and restarts has eroded process credibility and investor confidence
Local SME access to airport commercial opportunities and local procurement requirements in the concession agreement have not been transparently disclosed
Environmental and energy efficiency obligations aligned with EU Green Deal targets and Energy Community obligations appear absent from the known concession terms
recommendations: Commission an independent valuation by at least two internationally recognized firms using multiple methodologies (asset-based, income-based/DCF, and market-comparable), with results published in full before any cabinet or parliamentary vote
Immediately initiate formal consultations with representative trade unions as required by the Zakon o radu and EU accession commitments, including union representation on the tender oversight body

Publish a comprehensive value-for-money analysis comparing the concession model with a state-managed investment alternative (using IPA III, EBRD, or EIB financing), ensuring Parliament has both options before voting

Embed explicit worker protection clauses in the concession agreement aligned with EU Directive 2001/23/EC on Transfer of Undertakings: preservation of existing collective agreements for at least 3 years, no involuntary redundancies for 2 years post-transfer, and mandatory retraining programs
Include binding local content and SME procurement requirements (e.g., minimum 30% of non-aeronautical commercial space reserved for Montenegrin SMEs) in the concession agreement

Incorporate environmental performance obligations including carbon reduction targets, renewable energy deployment at airport facilities, and alignment with Montenegro's National Energy and Climate Plan

Ensure the concession fee structure captures upside from traffic growth through revenue-sharing mechanisms (e.g., progressive concession fee tied to passenger volumes above baseline), preventing the concessionaire from capturing all growth value over 30 years

counter_proposal.title: Transparent Dual-Track Airport Development Strategy
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than proceeding with a single-track concession process that has been compromised by delays, legal disputes, and lack of social dialogue, the Ministry of Economy proposes a dual-track approach: (1) pause the current tender for 90 days to complete an independent multi-firm valuation and publish a rigorous public sector comparator, and (2) present Parliament with two fully costed options -- the concession as restructured with enhanced worker protections and revenue-sharing, versus a state-managed modernization program financed through EU pre-accession funds and development bank loans. This ensures the decision is evidence-based, democratically legitimate, and maximizes long-term value.
key_changes: Impose a 90-day pause on the tender to complete independent valuation and public sector comparator analysis
Commission two independent international valuation firms (not solely the Administration for State Property) to assess airport value using DCF, asset-based, and market-comparable methods
Develop and publish a full public sector comparator showing costs and benefits of state-managed development using IPA III, EBRD, and EIB financing
Establish a tripartite oversight committee (government, unions, independent experts) to review and approve the final concession terms or alternative investment plan
Restructure the concession fee to include a progressive revenue-sharing mechanism that captures traffic growth upside for the state
Embed EU-standard worker protections (Transfer of Undertakings provisions, collective agreement continuity, retraining guarantees) as non-negotiable terms
Present Parliament with both options and full supporting analysis for an informed democratic decision
expected_benefits: Ensures Montenegro captures fair value for a rapidly appreciating strategic asset (current value likely EUR 300-400M vs. 2018 estimate of EUR 140M)
Restores process legitimacy and public trust by addressing union and opposition concerns through transparency and social dialogue
Provides Parliament with evidence-based options rather than a single fait accompli, strengthening democratic oversight
Protects 400+ airport workers' rights in line with EU Transfer of Undertakings standards and Chapter 19 obligations
Creates a revenue-sharing structure that allows the state to benefit from future traffic growth (projected to exceed 4M passengers by 2030)
Demonstrates EU accession readiness by aligning with PPP best practices identified by the EU Court of Auditors
Reduces litigation risk by addressing procedural deficiencies that have already generated legal challenges from bidders
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. A 90-day pause is operationally manageable given that the bidder deadline is May 9, 2026, allowing time for valuation and analysis without derailing the process entirely. Independent valuation firms can be engaged quickly through existing IFC advisory channels. The dual-track approach is well-precedented in EU member states (e.g., Portugal's airport concession process included a public sector comparator). The main political obstacle is the government's apparent preference for speed, but the alternative is a deal that faces legal challenges, union opposition, and potential parliamentary rejection -- all of which would cause far greater delays.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Transparentna strategija razvoja aerodroma po dvostrukom kolosijeku
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto nastavka procesa koncesije po jednom kolosijeku, koji je kompromitovan kašnjenjima, pravnim sporovima i odsustvom socijalnog dijaloga, Ministarstvo ekonomije predlaže pristup po dvostrukom kolosijeku: (1) pauza trenutnog tendera na 90 dana radi završetka nezavisne procjene vrijednosti od strane više firmi i objavljivanja rigoroznog komparatora javnog sektora, i (2) predstavljanje Parlamentu dvije potpuno finansijski razrađene opcije -- koncesija restrukturisana uz pojačanu zaštitu radnika i podjelu prihoda, nasuprot državno upravljanom programu modernizacije finansiranom kroz pretpristupne fondove EU i zajmove razvojnih banaka. Na taj način se osigurava da odluka bude zasnovana na dokazima, demokratski legitimna i da maksimizira dugoročnu vrijednost.
key_changes_mne: Uvesti pauzu od 90 dana na tender radi završetka nezavisne procjene vrijednosti i analize komparatora javnog sektora

Angažovati dvije nezavisne međunarodne firme za procjenu vrijednosti (ne isključivo Upravu za imovinu) kako bi se vrijednost aerodroma procijenila metodama DCF, na osnovu imovine i tržišnih uporedivih vrijednosti
Izraditi i objaviti kompletan komparator javnog sektora koji prikazuje troškove i koristi državno upravljanog razvoja uz korišćenje finansiranja IPA III, EBRD i EIB
Uspostaviti tripartitni nadzorni odbor (vlada, sindikati, nezavisni stručnjaci) radi pregleda i odobravanja konačnih uslova koncesije ili alternativnog investicionog plana
Restrukturisati koncesionu naknadu uvođenjem progresivnog mehanizma podjele prihoda koji za državu osigurava korist od rasta saobraćaja
Ugraditi zaštitu radnika po standardima EU (odredbe o prenosu preduzeća, kontinuitet kolektivnih ugovora, garancije prekvalifikacije) kao nepregovarajuće uslove
Predstaviti Parlamentu obje opcije s kompletnom pratećom analizom radi informisane demokratske odluke
expected_benefits_mne: Osigurava da Crna Gora dobije fer vrijednost za strateški asset koji rapidno raste (trenutna vrijednost je vjerovatno između 300 i 400 miliona EUR u poređenju sa procjenom iz 2018. godine od 140 miliona EUR)
Vraća legitimitet procesu i povjerenje javnosti rješavanjem zabrinutosti sindikata i opozicije kroz transparentnost i socijalni dijalog
Pruža Parlamentu opcije zasnovane na dokazima umjesto jednog gotovog čina, jačajući demokratski nadzor
Štiti prava 400+ radnika aerodroma u skladu sa standardima EU o prenosu preduzeća i obavezama iz Poglavlja 19
Stvara strukturu podjele prihoda koja omogućava državi da profitira od budućeg rasta saobraćaja (koji se projektuje da će premašiti 4 miliona putnika do 2030. godine)
Demonstrira spremnost za pristupanje EU usklađivanjem sa najboljim praksama JPP koje je identifikovao Revizorski sud EU
Smanjuje rizik od sudskih sporova otklanjanjem proceduralnih nedostataka koji su već doveli do pravnih izazova od strane ponuđača
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Pauza od 90 dana je operativno izvodljiva s obzirom na to da je rok za ponuđače 9. maj 2026. godine, što ostavlja dovoljno vremena za procjenu vrijednosti i analizu bez potpunog narušavanja procesa. Nezavisne firme za procjenu vrijednosti mogu biti angažovane brzo putem postojećih savjetodavnih kanala IFC-a. Pristup dvostrukog kolosijeka ima dobre presedane u državama članicama EU (npr. portugalski proces koncesije aerodroma uključivao je komparator javnog sektora). Glavna politička prepreka je očigledna sklonost vlade ka brzini, ali alternativa je sporazum koji se suočava s pravnim izazovima, opozicijom sindikata i potencijalnim parlamentarnim odbijanjem -- što bi sve prouzrokovalo znatno veća kašnjenja.

assessments.summary_mne: Aerodromska koncesija, ukoliko bude pravilno strukturirana, mogla bi privući značajne strane direktne investicije i modernizovati kritičnu saobraćajnu infrastrukturu, međutim proces je opterećen kašnjenjima, pravnim sporovima, nedostatkom socijalnog dijaloga i nedovoljnom transparentnošću u pogledu procjene vrijednosti imovine. Uz promet putnika koji je 2025. godine premašio 3 miliona i aerodrome koji ostvaruju sve veće prihode, Ministarstvo ekonomije izražava zabrinutost da Vlada možda potcjenjuje strateški udio koji brzo raste na vrijednosti. Izostanak smislenog savjetovanja sa radnicima, jednoglasno protivljenje sindikata i nepostojanje objavljene komparativne analize (koncesija nasuprot razvoja pod državnim upravljanjem) postavljaju ozbiljna pitanja o tome da li ovaj ugovor maksimizuje dugoročnu ekonomsku vrijednost za Crnu Goru.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Proces aerodromske koncesije ima potencijal da obezbijedi potrebne strane direktne investicije i investicije u infrastrukturu, međutim rizikuje da u značajnoj mjeri potcijeni strateški nacionalni udio čiji je promet rastao 15% godišnje. Najhitnije zabrinutosti odnose se na izostanak nezavisne i transparentne procjene vrijednosti koja odražava trenutne tržišne uslove, kao i na potpuno isključivanje radnika i sindikata iz procesa, uprkos njihovim zakonskim pravima na konsultacije. Ministarstvo preporučuje prekid tendera do okončanja nezavisne procjene zasnovan na više metoda i objave vjerodostojne komparativne analize koncesije nasuprot razvoja pod vođstvom države.
key_concerns_mne: Procjena vrijednosti imovine iz 2018. godine od 140 miliona eura ozbiljno je zastarjela s obzirom na rekordni rast prometa (3M+ putnika u 2025. godini), a novi proces procjene nema potvrđenu nezavisnost niti metodologiju zasnovanu na više pristupa

Sindikati radnika aerodroma su jednoglasno isključeni iz smislenog učešća u procesu koncesije, čime se krši duh crnogorskog radnog zakonodavstva, konvencija MOR-a i zahtjeva Poglavlja 19 o socijalnom dijalogu u okviru procesa pristupanja EU

Nije objavljena nikakva analiza javnog sektorskog komparatora niti analiza vrijednosti za novac kojom bi se upoređivao model koncesije sa razvojem pod državnim upravljanjem finansiranim iz pretpristupnih instrumenata EU ili razvojnih banaka

Odredbe o zaštiti radnika u nacrtu ugovora o koncesiji (garancije zarada, kontinuitet kolektivnog ugovora, zaštitne mjere protiv otpuštanja) i dalje su nedovoljno razrađene ili neobjavjene, što stvara pravnu nesigurnost u skladu sa Direktivom EU o prenosu preduzeća
Dugotrajni postupak tendera (koji traje od 2018–2019. godine), uz višestruke pravne osporavanja i ponovne pokretanje postupka, narušio je kredibilitet procesa i povjerenje investitora
Pristup lokalnih malih i srednjih preduzeća komercijalnim prilikama na aerodromu i zahtjevi za lokalnu nabavku u ugovoru o koncesiji nisu transparentno objelodanjeni

Obaveze u oblasti zaštite životne sredine i energetske efikasnosti usklađene sa ciljevima Evropskog zelenog plana i obavezama prema Energetskoj zajednici izgleda da su odsutne iz poznatih uslova koncesije
recommendations_mne: Naručiti nezavisnu procjenu od najmanje dvije međunarodno priznate firme, korišćenjem više metodologija (zasnovane na imovini, prihodima/DCF i tržišnim komparabilima), uz objavu potpunih rezultata prije bilo kakvog glasanja u Vladi ili Parlamentu

Odmah pokrenuti formalne konsultacije sa reprezentativnim sindikatima, u skladu sa zahtjevima Zakona o radu i obavezama iz procesa pristupanja EU, uključujući zastupljenost sindikata u tijelu za nadzor tendera

Objaviti sveobuhvatnu analizu vrijednosti za novac kojom se upoređuje model koncesije sa alternativom državno upravljanih investicija (uz korišćenje finansiranja iz IPA III, EBRD-a ili EIB-a), osiguravajući da Parlament ima uvid u obje opcije prije glasanja

Ugraditi izričite klauzule o zaštiti radnika u ugovor o koncesiji, usklađene sa Direktivom EU 2001/23/EZ o prenosu preduzeća: očuvanje postojećih kolektivnih ugovora na period od najmanje 3 godine, bez prinudnih viška radnika u trajanju od 2 godine nakon prenosa i obavezne programe prekvalifikacije

Uključiti obavezujuće zahtjeve za lokalni sadržaj i nabavku od malih i srednjih preduzeća (npr. minimum 30% neaeronautičkog komercijalnog prostora rezervisano za crnogorska mala i srednja preduzeća) u ugovor o koncesiji

Uključiti obaveze u pogledu ekoloških performansi, uključujući ciljeve smanjenja emisije ugljika, razvoj obnovljive energije u objektima aerodroma i usklađenost sa Nacionalnim energetskim i klimatskim planom Crne Gore

Osigurati da struktura koncesione naknade bilježi rast prometa kroz mehanizme podjele prihoda (npr. progresivna koncesiona naknada vezana za obim putnika iznad polazne osnove), sprečavajući da koncesionar prisvoji svu vrijednost rasta u narednih 30 godina

assessments.summary: The airport concession decision is primarily a transport and fiscal matter with indirect but significant implications for education. The potential upfront payment of EUR 100-200 million and ongoing concession fees represent a substantial fiscal event for a country with a GDP of roughly EUR 7 billion and education spending at approximately 4% of GDP. However, there is no indication that concession revenues have been earmarked or even discussed in relation to education investment. The proposed amendment shifting 80% of concession revenue from municipalities to the central budget could either benefit or harm education depending on allocation priorities, but the absence of any education-linked spending commitments is a missed opportunity. The 25-30 year concession period will span an entire generation of Montenegrin students, and the decision's effects on connectivity, tourism employment pathways, and municipal fiscal capacity will shape educational outcomes for decades.
assessments.executive_summary: The airport concession is education-neutral on its face but represents a missed opportunity to link major fiscal proceeds to Montenegro's chronically underfunded education system. The key concern is that the proposed law amendment centralizing 80% of concession revenue provides no earmarking for education, while the 25-30 year concession period will profoundly affect workforce pipeline needs, vocational training, and brain drain dynamics. The Ministry recommends that Parliament condition any concession approval on binding commitments to allocate a defined share of proceeds toward education infrastructure, VET aviation programs, and student mobility support.
assessments.reasoning:
The Ministry of Education assesses this decision through several interconnected lenses.

FISCAL IMPACT ON EDUCATION FUNDING: Montenegro spends approximately 4% of GDP on education, which is below the EU average of around 5%. The airport concession could generate an upfront payment of EUR 100-200 million plus ongoing annual fees over 25-30 years. For context, Montenegro's total annual education budget is roughly EUR 280 million. Even a modest earmarking of concession proceeds -- say 10-15% -- could meaningfully address chronic underfunding of school infrastructure, teacher salaries, and research. Yet no public discussion or legal provision links concession revenue to education. The proposed law amendment redirecting 80% of revenues from municipalities to the central state budget (from the current 70% municipal share) creates both risk and opportunity: centralized revenue could theoretically be directed toward national education priorities, but without earmarking, it is more likely to be absorbed into general fiscal consolidation or debt reduction.

MUNICIPAL EDUCATION IMPACT: Under current law, municipalities of Tivat and Zeta would receive 70% of concession revenues. Both municipalities fund local schools and educational facilities. The proposed reduction to 20% would significantly diminish their fiscal capacity. Tivat in particular, as a coastal tourism hub, relies on local revenue for school maintenance and educational programming. Reducing municipal concession income without compensating education transfers risks degrading local school quality in these areas.

VOCATIONAL EDUCATION AND WORKFORCE DEVELOPMENT: A 25-30 year airport concession creates long-term demand for skilled aviation workers -- ground handling, aircraft maintenance, air traffic management support, security, hospitality, and logistics. Montenegro's current VET framework does not include specialized aviation vocational tracks. Montenegro Airports operates a basic training center, but this is not integrated into the formal VET system. A well-structured concession agreement could require the concessionaire to invest in vocational training programs, apprenticeships, and partnerships with Montenegrin secondary schools and the University of Montenegro's engineering faculties. The current tender documents, as publicly reported, do not appear to include such workforce development obligations. This is a significant missed opportunity given that Montenegro's Youth Guarantee pilot (2025-2026) aims to connect young people with employment and training, and aviation represents a growth sector.

BRAIN DRAIN IMPLICATIONS: Approximately 50% of Montenegrin youth express desire to emigrate, and youth unemployment remains around 26%. Improved airport infrastructure and expanded air connectivity could paradoxically accelerate brain drain by making it easier and cheaper to leave. Conversely, a well-managed concession that generates local employment, attracts international companies, and creates career pathways could help retain talent. The education system must be positioned to benefit from whichever scenario unfolds. Without proactive VET alignment and higher education partnerships, the concession risks primarily creating low-skill jobs while higher-skill positions are filled by expatriates, as has occurred in other Western Balkan concession and privatization processes.

HIGHER EDUCATION AND RESEARCH: The concession has limited direct implications for higher education, but there are indirect effects worth noting. Improved airport connectivity could support the internationalization of the University of Montenegro and private universities by facilitating student and researcher mobility -- relevant for Erasmus+ participation and Bologna Process alignment. However, this benefit depends entirely on the concessionaire's route development strategy and pricing. If the concessionaire focuses exclusively on tourism charter routes (as is likely given Tivat's seasonal demand), academic mobility may not improve significantly.

EU ACCESSION CONTEXT: Montenegro's EU accession process includes education benchmarks under Chapter 26 (Education and Culture). The European Commission's 2024 Montenegro Report noted continued challenges in education quality, infrastructure, and alignment with EU standards. The EUR 11 million EU grant for education infrastructure (via EIB, announced 2024) and the WBIF education infrastructure improvement program (EUR 64 million target) demonstrate that the EU prioritizes Montenegrin education investment. A large concession deal that generates fiscal revenue without directing any toward education would be inconsistent with the reform trajectory expected by EU partners.

TRANSPARENCY AND PROCESS CONCERNS: The Airport Syndicate's call for independent, transparent, and publicly verifiable asset valuation is educationally relevant insofar as it reflects broader governance standards. Strong institutional governance -- transparent processes, independent oversight, parliamentary scrutiny -- models the kind of civic accountability that education systems should both teach and exemplify. The Ministry supports the principle that the valuation must be rigorous and public, and that Parliament must exercise meaningful oversight if the value exceeds the EUR 150 million threshold.
key_concerns: No earmarking of concession revenues for education despite chronic underfunding at 4% of GDP, well below EU average
Proposed law amendment reducing municipal share from 70% to 20% will weaken local education funding capacity in Tivat and Zeta without guaranteed compensating transfers
Absence of workforce development and VET obligations in the concession tender, missing the opportunity to build aviation vocational pathways for Montenegrin youth
Risk that improved air connectivity accelerates brain drain without compensating education and employment retention measures
25-30 year concession duration locks in terms for an entire generation with no apparent mechanism for education-sector input or periodic review of social impact obligations
Concessionaire route strategy may prioritize tourism charters over academic mobility connections relevant to Erasmus+ and university internationalization
recommendations: Parliament should condition concession approval on earmarking at least 10% of all concession revenues (upfront and annual) for an Education and Skills Development Fund, ring-fenced for school infrastructure, VET programs, and teacher training

The concession agreement should include binding workforce development obligations requiring the concessionaire to fund aviation VET programs, apprenticeships for at least 50 Montenegrin students annually, and partnerships with the University of Montenegro

The law amendment on revenue distribution should guarantee that municipalities of Tivat and Zeta maintain current per-capita education funding levels regardless of the new revenue split, through compensating central transfers if necessary

Include a mandatory mid-term social impact review (at year 12-15 of the concession) with education sector participation, allowing adjustment of workforce development and community investment obligations
Negotiate route development commitments that include year-round connections to major European university cities to support Erasmus+ mobility and reduce brain drain by ensuring returnability
Integrate the airport concession workforce pipeline into the Youth Guarantee pilot program (2025-2026) to channel NEET youth into aviation vocational training and apprenticeships

counter_proposal.title: Education-Linked Airport Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Education proposes that the airport concession proceed but with binding education and workforce development conditions embedded in the concession agreement and the enabling legislation. Rather than treating the concession as a pure transport-fiscal transaction, it should be structured as a generational investment vehicle that simultaneously upgrades aviation infrastructure and strengthens Montenegro's human capital pipeline. This requires earmarked revenue, mandatory VET partnerships, and social impact accountability mechanisms.
key_changes: Establish a statutory Education and Skills Fund receiving 10% of all concession payments, administered jointly by the Ministry of Education and Ministry of Finance
Require the concessionaire to create an Aviation Academy or VET partnership with existing Montenegrin educational institutions within 3 years of concession start
Mandate minimum annual apprenticeship quotas (50 positions) across airport operations, maintenance, hospitality, and management
Preserve municipal education funding floors for Tivat and Zeta through a compensating transfer mechanism in the amended revenue distribution law
Include route development KPIs that incentivize year-round connectivity to EU education hubs alongside seasonal tourism routes
Build a 15-year social impact review clause with education sector representation on the review panel
expected_benefits: Direct education funding stream of approximately EUR 10-20 million upfront plus annual revenues over the concession period
Creation of Montenegro's first formal aviation VET pathway, addressing a gap in the current vocational education framework
Reduced youth unemployment and NEET rates through targeted apprenticeship pipeline in a growth sector
Brain drain mitigation through local career pathways and improved academic mobility connections
Alignment with EU accession education benchmarks and demonstration of integrated policy-making to European Commission
Protection of local education quality in airport-adjacent municipalities during the revenue redistribution
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. Education-linked concession conditions are standard practice in EU member states and are explicitly encouraged by the European Commission for EU candidate countries. The IFC, which is advising on this concession, regularly includes social impact requirements in infrastructure PPP frameworks. The 10% revenue earmark is modest relative to total concession value and would not materially affect bidder interest. VET partnership obligations are common in airport concessions globally (examples include Vienna, Zagreb, and Skopje airports). The main implementation challenge is inter-ministerial coordination between Transport, Finance, and Education, but this is a governance issue rather than a structural barrier.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir za aerodromsku koncesiju povezanu s obrazovanjem
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo prosvjete predlaže da koncesija aerodroma bude odobrena, ali uz obavezujuće uslove za obrazovanje i razvoj radne snage ugrađene u ugovor o koncesiji i pratično zakonodavstvo. Umjesto da se koncesija tretira kao čista transportno-fiskalna transakcija, treba je strukturirati kao generacijsko investiciono vozilo koje istovremeno unapređuje avioinfrastrukturu i jača pipeline ljudskog kapitala Crne Gore. To zahtijeva namjenski raspoređene prihode, obavezna partnerstva u stručnom obrazovanju i mehanizme odgovornosti za društveni uticaj.
key_changes_mne: Uspostaviti zakonski Fond za obrazovanje i vještine koji prima 10% svih koncesionih plaćanja, kojim zajednički upravljaju Ministarstvo prosvjete i Ministarstvo finansija
Obavezati koncesionara da osnuje Avijacijsku akademiju ili partnerstvo sa strukovnim obrazovanjem uz postojeće crnogorske obrazovne institucije u roku od 3 godine od početka koncesije
Propisati minimalne godišnje kvote za pripravnike (50 mjesta) u oblasti aerodromskih operacija, tehničkog održavanja, ugostiteljstva i menadžmenta
Zaštititi minimalne nivoe finansiranja opštinskog obrazovanja za Tivat i Zetu putem kompenzacionog mehanizma prenosa u izmijenjenom zakonu o raspodjeli prihoda
Uključiti KPI-eve za razvoj ruta koji podstiču cjelogodišnju povezanost s obrazovnim čvorištima u EU uz sezonske turističke rute
Ugraditi klauzulu o petnaestogodišnjoj reviziji društvenog uticaja uz učešće predstavnika obrazovnog sektora u revizionoj komisiji
expected_benefits_mne: Direktan tok finansiranja obrazovanja u iznosu od aproximativno 10–20 miliona EUR unaprijed, uz godišnje prihode tokom trajanja koncesije
Uspostavljanje prvog formalnog puta strukovnog obrazovanja u avijaciji u Crnoj Gori, čime se popunjava praznina u trenutnom okviru stručnog obrazovanja
Smanjenje nezaposlenosti mladih i stope NEET-a kroz ciljani pipeline pripravništva u sektoru rasta
Ublažavanje odliva mozgova kroz lokalne karijerne putanje i poboljšane veze za akademsku mobilnost
Usklađenost s obrazovnim referentnim vrijednostima za pristupanje EU i demonstracija integrisanog kreiranja politika prema Evropskoj komisiji
Zaštita kvaliteta lokalnog obrazovanja u opštinama u blizini aerodroma tokom preraspodjele prihoda
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Uslovi koncesije vezani za obrazovanje standardna su praksa u državama članicama EU i Evropska komisija ih eksplicitno podstiče za kandidate za pristupanje EU. IFC, koji savjetuje u ovoj koncesiji, redovno uključuje zahtjeve za društveni uticaj u PPP okvire za infrastrukturu. Namjensko izdvajanje od 10% prihoda skromno je u odnosu na ukupnu vrijednost koncesije i neće materijalno uticati na interesovanje ponuđača. Obaveze partnerstva u strukovnom obrazovanju uobičajene su u aerodromskim koncesijama širom svijeta (primjeri uključuju aerodrome Beč, Zagreb i Skoplje). Glavni izazov u implementaciji je međuresorska koordinacija između ministarstava saobraćaja, finansija i prosvjete, ali to je pitanje upravljanja, a ne strukturna prepreka.

assessments.summary_mne: Odluka o koncesiji aerodroma primarno je transportno i fiskalno pitanje s indirektnim, ali značajnim implikacijama za obrazovanje. Potencijalna jednokratna uplata od 100–200 miliona EUR i tekuće naknade za koncesiju predstavljaju značajan fiskalni događaj za zemlju s BDP-om od otprilike 7 milijardi EUR i rashodima za obrazovanje od oko 4% BDP-a. Međutim, ne postoje naznake da su prihodi od koncesije namijenjeni ili čak razmatrani u kontekstu ulaganja u obrazovanje. Predloženi amandman kojim se 80% prihoda od koncesije prenosi s opština na centralni budžet može imati povoljne ili štetne efekte po obrazovanje, ovisno o prioritetima raspodjele, ali odsustvo bilo kakvih obaveza vezanih za obrazovne rashode propuštena je prilika. Period koncesije od 25–30 godina obuhvatiće čitavu generaciju crnogorskih učenika, a efekti ove odluke na povezanost, puteve zapošljavanja u turizmu i fiskalnu sposobnost opština oblikovat će obrazovne ishode decenijama.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Koncesija aerodroma naizgled je neutralna po pitanju obrazovanja, ali predstavlja propuštenu priliku da se značajni fiskalni prihodi povežu s hronično nedovoljno finansiranim obrazovnim sistemom Crne Gore. Ključna briga odnosi se na to da predloženi zakonski amandman kojim se centralizuje 80% prihoda od koncesije ne predviđa namjensko raspoređivanje sredstava za obrazovanje, dok će period koncesije od 25–30 godina u velikoj mjeri uticati na potrebe kadrovskog procesa, stručnu obuku i dinamiku odljeva mozgova. Ministarstvo preporučuje da Parlament kao uslov za bilo koje odobrenje koncesije postavi obavezujuće obaveze o dodjeli definisanog udjela prihoda za obrazovnu infrastrukturu, vazduhoplovne SOO programe i podršku mobilnosti studenata.
key_concerns_mne: Nema namjenskog raspoređivanja prihoda od koncesije za obrazovanje uprkos hroničnom nedovoljnom finansiranju od 4% BDP-a, što je znatno ispod prosjeka EU
Predloženi zakonski amandman kojim se udio opština smanjuje sa 70% na 20% oslabljuje lokalnu sposobnost finansiranja obrazovanja u Tivtu i Zeti bez zagarantovanih kompenzacionih transfera

Odsustvo obaveza vezanih za razvoj radne snage i stručno obrazovanje i osposobljavanje (SOO) u tenderu za koncesiju, čime se propušta prilika za izgradnju vazduhoplovnih stručnih putanja za crnogorsku omladinu
Rizik da poboljšana vazdušna povezanost ubrza odljev mozgova bez kompenzacionih mjera za zadržavanje kroz obrazovanje i zapošljavanje
Trajanje koncesije od 25–30 godina fiksira uslove za čitavu jednu generaciju bez ikakvog vidljivog mehanizma za učešće obrazovnog sektora ili periodičnu reviziju obaveza u pogledu društvenog uticaja
Strategija rutnih pravaca koncesionara može davati prednost turističkim čarter letovima nauštrb veza akademske mobilnosti relevantnih za Erasmus+ i internacionalizaciju univerziteta
recommendations_mne: Parlament bi trebalo da uspostavi uslov za odobrenje koncesije koji podrazumijeva namjensko izdvajanje najmanje 10% svih prihoda od koncesije (jednokratnih i godišnjih) za Fond za obrazovanje i razvoj vještina, strogo namijenjen za školsku infrastrukturu, programe SOO i obuku nastavnika

Ugovor o koncesiji trebalo bi da sadrži obavezujuće odredbe o razvoju radne snage kojima se od koncesionara zahtijeva finansiranje programa vazduhoplovnog SOO, stručne prakse za najmanje 50 crnogorskih studenata godišnje, te partnerstva s Univerzitetom Crne Gore

Zakonski amandman o raspodjeli prihoda trebalo bi da garantuje da opštine Tivat i Zeta zadržavaju trenutni nivo finansiranja obrazovanja po glavi stanovnika bez obzira na novu raspodjelu prihoda, putem kompenzacionih centralnih transfera ukoliko je to potrebno

Uvesti obaveznu reviziju društvenog uticaja u sredini trajanja ugovora (između 12. i 15. godine koncesije) uz učešće obrazovnog sektora, uz mogućnost prilagođavanja obaveza u pogledu razvoja radne snage i ulaganja u zajednicu

Pregovarati o obavezama za razvoj ruta koje uključuju veze tokom cijele godine s glavnim evropskim univerzitetskim gradovima radi podrške Erasmus+ mobilnosti i smanjenja odljeva mozgova kroz osiguranje mogućnosti povratka

Integrisati kadar iz kadrovskog procesa koncesije aerodroma u pilot program Garancije za mlade (2025–2026) kako bi se mladi koji nijesu u sistemu obrazovanja, zaposlenja ni obuke (NEET) usmjerili u stručno vazduhoplovno obrazovanje i praksu

assessments.summary: The airport concession decision is primarily an infrastructure and fiscal matter with indirect but non-trivial implications for public health. Airports serve as critical nodes for medical evacuation of patients requiring specialist care unavailable in Montenegro, for pharmaceutical supply chains, and for the mobility of health workers. The concession itself is neither inherently harmful nor beneficial to health -- what matters is how concession terms address health-related obligations (accessibility for persons with reduced mobility, medical evacuation priority, uninterrupted pharmaceutical cargo logistics) and whether concession revenues are channelled toward underfunded healthcare and social protection systems. The current process lacks any visible health impact assessment or health-sector consultation.
assessments.executive_summary: This decision is health-neutral on its face but carries significant indirect risks: Montenegro's airports are essential for medical evacuations, pharmaceutical logistics, and health workforce mobility, yet no health impact assessment or health-sector consultation appears in the concession process. The Ministry of Health recommends mandatory health-related clauses in any concession agreement and earmarking of a portion of concession revenues for healthcare system strengthening.
assessments.reasoning:
The airport concession decision, while primarily falling under the domains of transport, finance, and economic policy, has several intersections with public health that the current process appears to overlook entirely.

MEDICAL EVACUATION AND PATIENT TRANSFER: Montenegro's healthcare system has well-documented capacity limitations, with the Clinical Center of Montenegro in Podgorica unable to provide certain specialist treatments (advanced oncology, complex cardiac surgery, organ transplants, paediatric subspecialties). Both Podgorica and Tivat airports serve as critical departure points for medical evacuations -- air ambulance services operate from these airports to transfer critically ill patients to facilities in Belgrade, Ljubljana, Rome, Vienna, and other European centres. Any concession agreement must guarantee uninterrupted priority access for medical evacuation flights, including helicopter ambulance operations from Tivat. A private concessionaire driven by commercial objectives could, without explicit contractual safeguards, deprioritize medical flights in favour of commercial traffic, particularly during peak tourist season when Tivat handles up to 50 active routes.

PHARMACEUTICAL AND MEDICAL SUPPLY LOGISTICS: Montenegro imports the vast majority of its pharmaceuticals and medical devices. Air cargo through Podgorica airport is part of the supply chain for time-sensitive medical products (vaccines requiring cold chain, blood products, emergency medications, transplant organs in transit). The concession agreement must include service-level requirements ensuring pharmaceutical cargo handling meets EU Good Distribution Practice (GDP) standards and is not degraded by the concessionaire's commercial priorities.

DISABILITY ACCESSIBILITY AND EU ALIGNMENT: As an EU candidate country, Montenegro is working toward alignment with the EU acquis, including Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 on the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility in air travel. The concession agreement for a 25-30 year term must mandate progressive alignment with these standards, including adequate PRM (Persons with Reduced Mobility) assistance infrastructure at both airports. Currently, neither airport fully meets EU accessibility standards. A concession that does not embed accessibility investment requirements would lock in substandard conditions for decades, contradicting Montenegro's deinstitutionalisation agenda and disability rights commitments.

HEALTH WORKFORCE MOBILITY (BRAIN DRAIN): Montenegro suffers from severe health workforce shortages -- it has among the lowest ratios of pharmacists per capita in Europe (17 per 100,000) and loses significant numbers of doctors and nurses to EU countries. Airport connectivity directly affects health worker migration patterns. While the concession may improve connectivity and make Montenegro more accessible (potentially attracting visiting specialists or medical tourists), it could equally facilitate further outmigration of health professionals if working conditions in the Montenegrin health system remain uncompetitive. This is not a direct consequence of the concession, but the broader policy coherence question is relevant: if concession revenues are not partly reinvested in health workforce retention, improved connectivity could accelerate brain drain.

WORKER SOCIAL PROTECTION: The airport unions (Sindikat Aerodroma Crne Gore) have expressed unanimous opposition to the concession, citing concerns about job security for current employees. From a social protection perspective, the concession agreement must guarantee continuity of employment, pension contributions to the Fond PIO, and health insurance coverage for transferred workers. Any disruption to social security contributions during a transition period would directly impact the health insurance system managed by RFZO.

REVENUE ALLOCATION AND HEALTH FINANCING: Montenegro's health system is chronically underfunded. The concession could generate significant upfront and annual revenues (reports suggest EUR 100 million upfront and EUR 200 million in investment commitments from the leading bidder). However, there is no indication that any portion of concession revenues is earmarked for healthcare or social protection. The government has indicated revenues may go to strategic infrastructure projects and municipal budgets, with no mention of health sector allocation. Given that the 2018 valuation was EUR 140 million and property values have roughly doubled since then, the fresh valuation could significantly exceed EUR 150 million, triggering parliamentary review -- which would at least create an opportunity for health policy advocates to influence revenue allocation.

ABSENCE OF HEALTH IMPACT ASSESSMENT: Perhaps most concerning from the Ministry's perspective is the complete absence of any health impact assessment in the concession process. The IFC-assisted tender and government deliberations appear to have proceeded without any formal consultation with health sector stakeholders. For a 25-30 year concession affecting critical national infrastructure, this is a significant oversight.
key_concerns: No health impact assessment has been conducted for a 25-30 year concession affecting critical medical evacuation and pharmaceutical logistics infrastructure
Concession terms may not guarantee priority access for medical evacuation flights, particularly during peak tourist season at Tivat airport
No provisions visible for ensuring pharmaceutical and medical supply chain continuity and cold-chain compliance under private management

Disability accessibility standards at both airports are below EU Regulation 1107/2006 requirements, and a long-term concession without mandatory accessibility investment could lock in substandard conditions for decades
Airport workers face social protection risks during ownership transition, including potential disruption to health insurance (RFZO) and pension (Fond PIO) contributions
No earmarking of concession revenues for healthcare system strengthening despite chronic underfunding of the health sector
Improved air connectivity under private management could accelerate health workforce brain drain if not accompanied by health worker retention policies
recommendations: Mandate a formal Health Impact Assessment before the concession agreement is finalized, with findings presented during parliamentary review
Insert binding clauses in the concession agreement guaranteeing 24/7 priority access for medical evacuation and air ambulance flights at both airports, with penalties for non-compliance
Require the concessionaire to maintain pharmaceutical cold-chain cargo handling capacity compliant with EU Good Distribution Practice standards
Include progressive disability accessibility investment targets aligned with EU Regulation 1107/2006, with milestones every 5 years throughout the concession period
Guarantee continuity of RFZO health insurance and Fond PIO pension contributions for all transferred airport employees, with a minimum 3-year employment protection clause
Earmark at least 10% of annual concession fees for health sector investment, specifically health workforce retention programmes and medical equipment procurement
Require the concessionaire to provide reduced-rate or free terminal space for telemedicine and emergency medical coordination facilities at both airports
Ensure parliamentary debate on the concession includes testimony from the Ministry of Health and the RFZO on health infrastructure dependencies

counter_proposal.title: Health-Proofed Airport Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than opposing or fundamentally altering the concession process, the Ministry of Health proposes a Health-Proofed Concession Framework that would embed mandatory health safeguards into the concession agreement before it proceeds to parliamentary review. This includes a health impact assessment, binding medical evacuation access clauses, pharmaceutical logistics guarantees, disability accessibility investment schedule, worker social protection continuity provisions, and earmarked health revenue allocation. The framework would ensure that a 25-30 year private concession does not degrade Montenegro's health infrastructure dependencies while potentially improving them through contractual obligations on the concessionaire.
key_changes: Add a mandatory health impact assessment as a prerequisite before parliamentary vote on the concession
Insert an irrevocable Medical Priority Access Protocol into the concession contract guaranteeing air ambulance and medical evacuation operations
Require EU-standard pharmaceutical cold-chain handling facilities as a concession condition
Establish a binding 5-year disability accessibility investment schedule aligned with EU Regulation 1107/2006
Create a Health and Social Protection Fund receiving 10% of annual concession fees, ring-fenced for health workforce retention and medical equipment
Mandate a 3-year minimum employment guarantee with full RFZO and Fond PIO contribution continuity for all transferred airport staff
expected_benefits: Guaranteed continuity of medical evacuation services for critically ill patients who cannot be treated in Montenegro
Protection of pharmaceutical supply chains critical to the national health system
Progressive improvement of airport accessibility for persons with disabilities, supporting Montenegro's EU accession and deinstitutionalisation goals
Dedicated revenue stream for addressing Montenegro's health workforce crisis and medical equipment gaps
Social protection continuity for airport workers, preventing disruption to the RFZO and Fond PIO systems
Stronger parliamentary scrutiny of a major concession through health-sector evidence and testimony
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. These measures do not require abandoning or fundamentally restructuring the concession process. They involve adding health-specific clauses to an agreement that is still being drafted, conducting an assessment that can be completed within the existing timeline before parliamentary review, and earmarking a modest portion of expected revenues. The main political obstacle would be resistance from bidders to additional contractual obligations, but health and safety requirements are standard in EU airport concession frameworks and would strengthen Montenegro's EU accession positioning.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir Koncesije sa Ugrađenim Zdravstvenim Zaštitnim Mjerama
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se suprotstavlja ili suštinski mijenja proces koncesije, Ministarstvo zdravlja predlaže Okvir Koncesije sa Ugrađenim Zdravstvenim Zaštitnim Mjerama, koji bi ugradio obavezne zdravstvene zaštitne mjere u ugovor o koncesiji prije nego što isti bude upućen na parlamentarnu raspravu. Ovo uključuje procjenu uticaja na zdravlje, obavezujuće klauzule o pristupu medicinskoj evakuaciji, garancije farmaceutske logistike, plan ulaganja u pristupačnost za osobe sa invaliditetom, odredbe o kontinuitetu socijalne zaštite radnika i namjensku raspodjelu zdravstvenih prihoda. Okvir bi osigurao da 25–30-godišnja privatna koncesija ne naruši zavisnost Crne Gore od zdravstvene infrastrukture, već da je potencijalno unaprijedi kroz ugovorne obaveze koncesionara.
key_changes_mne: Uvesti obaveznu procjenu uticaja na zdravlje kao preduslov prije parlamentarnog glasanja o koncesiji
Ugraditi neopozivi Protokol o medicinskom prioritetnom pristupu u ugovor o koncesiji, kojim se garantuju operacije vazdušne hitne pomoći i medicinske evakuacije
Uvesti kao uslov koncesije objekte za rukovanje farmaceutskim hladnim lancem u skladu sa EU standardima
Uspostaviti obavezujući 5-godišnji plan ulaganja u pristupačnost za osobe sa invaliditetom, usklađen sa EU Uredbom 1107/2006
Osnovati Fond za zdravlje i socijalnu zaštitu koji prima 10% godišnjih naknada za koncesiju, namjenski rezervisano za zadržavanje zdravstvenih radnika i medicinsku opremu
Propisati minimalnu garanciju zaposlenja u trajanju od 3 godine sa punim kontinuitetom doprinosa RFZO-u i Fondu PIO za sve preuzete radnike aerodroma
expected_benefits_mne: Zagarantovani kontinuitet usluga medicinske evakuacije za teško oboljele pacijente koji ne mogu biti liječeni u Crnoj Gori
Zaštita lanaca snabdijevanja farmaceutskim proizvodima koji su od ključnog značaja za nacionalni zdravstveni sistem
Postepeno unapređenje pristupačnosti aerodroma za osobe sa invaliditetom, u prilog ciljevima Crne Gore vezanim za pristupanje EU i deinstitucionalizaciju
Namjenski tok prihoda za rješavanje krize zdravstvenih radnika i nedostatka medicinske opreme u Crnoj Gori
Kontinuitet socijalne zaštite radnika aerodroma, čime se sprečava poremećaj sistema RFZO i Fonda PIO
Snažniji parlamentarni nadzor nad značajnom koncesijom kroz dokaze i svjedočenja iz sektora zdravstva
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Ove mjere ne zahtijevaju odustajanje od procesa koncesije niti njegovo suštinsko restrukturiranje. One podrazumijevaju dodavanje klauzula specifičnih za zdravstvo u ugovor koji je još uvijek u fazi izrade, sprovođenje procjene koja može biti završena u okviru postojećeg vremenskog okvira prije parlamentarne rasprave, te namjensko izdvajanje skromnog dijela očekivanih prihoda. Glavna politička prepreka bila bi otpor ponuđača prema dodatnim ugovornim obavezama, međutim zahtjevi u pogledu zdravlja i bezbjednosti su standardni u okvirima EU za koncesije aerodroma i ojačali bi pozicioniranje Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU.

assessments.summary_mne: Odluka o koncesiji aerodroma primarno je infrastrukturno i fiskalno pitanje s posrednim, ali ne i zanemarljivim implikacijama po javno zdravlje. Aerodromi predstavljaju ključne čvorišta za medicinsku evakuaciju pacijenata kojima je potrebna specijalistička njega nedostupna u Crnoj Gori, za lance snabdijevanja lijekovima, kao i za mobilnost zdravstvenih radnika. Sama koncesija po sebi nije ni štetna ni korisna po zdravlje — ono što je važno jeste na koji način uslovi koncesije regulišu obaveze vezane za zdravlje (pristupačnost za osobe sa smanjenom pokretljivošću, prioritet medicinske evakuacije, neprekinuta logistika farmaceutskog tereta) i da li se prihodi od koncesije usmjeravaju ka nedovoljno finansiranim sistemima zdravstvene zaštite i socijalne zaštite. Aktuelni proces ne sadrži nikakvu vidljivu procjenu uticaja na zdravlje niti konsultacije sa zdravstvenim sektorom.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ova odluka je naizgled neutralna po zdravlje, ali nosi značajne posredne rizike: crnogorski aerodromi od suštinskog su značaja za medicinsku evakuaciju, farmaceutsku logistiku i mobilnost zdravstvenih radnika, a ipak se u procesu koncesije ne pojavljuje nikakva procjena uticaja na zdravlje niti konsultacija s zdravstvenim sektorom. Ministarstvo zdravlja preporučuje uvođenje obaveznih klauzula vezanih za zdravlje u svaki ugovor o koncesiji, kao i namjensko izdvajanje dijela prihoda od koncesije za jačanje zdravstvenog sistema.
key_concerns_mne: Nije sprovedena procjena uticaja na zdravlje za koncesiju od 25–30 godina koja se tiče kritične infrastrukture medicinske evakuacije i farmaceutske logistike
Uslovi koncesije možda ne garantuju prioritetni pristup za letove medicinske evakuacije, naročito tokom turističke sezone na aerodromu Tivat
Ne postoje vidljive odredbe kojima bi se osigurali kontinuitet farmaceutskog i medicinskog lanca snabdijevanja te usklađenost s hladnim lancem pod privatnim upravljanjem

Standardi pristupačnosti za osobe s invaliditetom na oba aerodroma ispod su zahtjeva Uredbe EU 1107/2006, a dugoročna koncesija bez obaveznih ulaganja u pristupačnost mogla bi učvrstiti ispodprosječne uslove na nekoliko decenija

Aerodromski radnici suočeni su s rizicima po socijalnu zaštitu tokom tranzicije vlasništva, uključujući potencijalni prekid uplata doprinosa za zdravstveno osiguranje (RFZO) i penzijsko osiguranje (Fond PIO)
Ne postoji namjensko izdvajanje prihoda od koncesije za jačanje zdravstvenog sistema, uprkos hroničnom nedofinansiranju zdravstvenog sektora
Poboljšana vazdušna povezanost pod privatnim upravljanjem mogla bi ubrzati odliv zdravstvenih kadrova u inostranstvo ukoliko ne bude praćena politikama zadržavanja zdravstvenih radnika
recommendations_mne: Obavezati na sprovođenje formalne Procjene uticaja na zdravlje prije finalizacije ugovora o koncesiji, uz prezentaciju nalaza tokom parlamentarne rasprave

Ugraditi obavezujuće klauzule u ugovor o koncesiji kojima se garantuje 24-satni prioritetni pristup za letove medicinske evakuacije i vazdušne ambulante na oba aerodroma, uz predviđene kazne za nepoštovanje
