Obavezati koncesionara da održava kapacitet za rukovanje farmaceutskim teretom u hladnom lancu u skladu sa standardima EU Dobre distributivne prakse
Uključiti progresivne ciljeve ulaganja u pristupačnost za osobe s invaliditetom usklađene s Uredbom EU 1107/2006, s prekretnicama svakih 5 godina tokom trajanja koncesije
Garantovati kontinuitet uplata zdravstvenog osiguranja RFZO i penzijskog osiguranja Fonda PIO za sve prenesene aerodromske radnike, uz minimalnu klauzulu zaštite zaposlenja od 3 godine
Namjenski izdvojiti najmanje 10% godišnjih naknada od koncesije za ulaganje u zdravstveni sektor, konkretno za programe zadržavanja zdravstvenih kadrova i nabavku medicinske opreme
Obavezati koncesionara da obezbijedi povlašćen ili besplatan terminalski prostor za telemedicinu i objekte za koordinaciju hitne medicinske pomoći na oba aerodroma
Osigurati da parlamentarna rasprava o koncesiji uključuje svjedočenja Ministarstva zdravlja i RFZO o zavisnostima zdravstvene infrastrukture

assessments.summary: The airport concession process advancing toward parliamentary review raises significant environmental concerns that appear largely unaddressed in the current framing of the decision. Tivat Airport sits adjacent to Tivatska Solila, a Ramsar-listed wetland of international importance and one of the last halophyte habitats on the eastern Adriatic, home to 178 bird species and 21 endemic species. A 25-30 year concession with expansion and capacity-boosting objectives will inevitably intensify pressure on this ecologically critical site, on the UNESCO-listed Bay of Kotor, and on Montenegro's fragile coastal zone. The government's focus on asset valuation and financial terms, while neglecting to publicly articulate binding environmental safeguards within the concession agreement, represents a missed opportunity and a potential threat to EU Chapter 27 alignment. Without robust environmental conditionality, Montenegro risks locking in decades of environmentally damaging airport operations at odds with its EU accession trajectory and climate commitments.
assessments.executive_summary: The airport concession process is proceeding with a concerning lack of visible environmental safeguards, despite Tivat Airport's proximity to the Ramsar-listed Tivatska Solila wetland and the UNESCO Bay of Kotor. The Ministry of Environment's top concern is that a 25-30 year agreement without binding environmental clauses could lock Montenegro into infrastructure expansion incompatible with EU Chapter 27 requirements and climate goals. We recommend halting the concession process until a comprehensive Strategic Environmental Assessment is completed and enforceable environmental conditions are embedded in the concession agreement.
assessments.reasoning:
The government's decision to advance the airport concession process by seeking a fresh asset valuation is framed almost entirely in financial and political terms -- asset value, parliamentary thresholds, bidder timelines, and political controversy over privatization. From an environmental perspective, several critical issues are either absent or insufficiently addressed.

First, the proximity of Tivat Airport to Tivatska Solila, designated as a Special Nature Reserve in 2008 and inscribed on the Ramsar List of Wetlands of International Importance in 2013, creates an inherent tension. The salt flats lie between the airport and the Lustica peninsula and serve as the last major resting and feeding ground for migratory birds along the Adriatic Flyway. Any airport expansion or intensification of operations over a 25-30 year concession period -- which is the explicit objective of the concession -- will increase noise, light pollution, habitat disturbance, and risk of bird strikes, all threatening this irreplaceable ecosystem. The reserve hosts 178 bird species, 21 endemic species, and some of the last halophyte vegetation on the eastern Adriatic coast. Yet there is no public indication that the concession agreement contains binding protections for Tivatska Solila.

Second, Tivat Airport serves as the primary aerial gateway to the Bay of Kotor (Boka Kotorska), a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979. UNESCO has previously raised concerns about development pressures in the Kotor region (UNESCO State of Conservation report, 2009). A concession designed to boost passenger throughput and expand airport infrastructure will accelerate tourism pressure on the Bay, compounding existing problems of illegal construction, uncontrolled urbanization, and inadequate wastewater infrastructure along the coast. The concession's spatial planning implications for the coastal zone appear unexamined.

Third, Montenegro is in accession negotiations with the EU and Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change) is among the most demanding chapters. The EU's aviation emissions framework requires full integration into the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS), with free allowances for aircraft operators being eliminated entirely by 2026. The UNDP has noted that both Montenegrin airports are only in the initial mapping phase of Airport Carbon Accreditation. A long-term concession that does not bind the concessionaire to EU-aligned emissions targets, sustainability reporting, and decarbonization milestones risks creating a structural obstacle to Chapter 27 closure. The IFC, which advised on the concession, follows its own Performance Standards on environmental and social sustainability, but the degree to which these have been reflected in the concession terms remains opaque.

Fourth, the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) and Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) frameworks under Montenegrin law (Law on Environmental Impact Assessment, Law on Strategic Environmental Assessment) require comprehensive assessment of major infrastructure concessions. While an earlier SEA was conducted for the Tivat Airport Urban Development Plan focusing primarily on noise impacts, this assessment dates back to 2016 and does not reflect updated passenger projections, climate commitments, or the cumulative impacts of a multi-decade concession. A new, comprehensive SEA should be mandatory before the concession proceeds to Parliament.

Fifth, the 4th Environmental Performance Review of Montenegro by UNECE (2024) identified persistent gaps in environmental governance, enforcement capacity, and integration of environmental considerations into economic decision-making. The airport concession exemplifies this pattern: a major economic decision with significant environmental implications being advanced on a financial and political timeline, with environmental considerations treated as secondary.

The financial focus on whether the valuation exceeds 150 million euros and thus triggers parliamentary approval is legitimate but insufficient. The environmental stakes of a 25-30 year commitment are at least as consequential as the financial ones. The absence of publicly articulated environmental conditionality in the concession suggests that environmental protection is not being treated as a core dimension of this deal.

Sources consulted include the Highways Today report on the concession tender (January 2025), SeeNews reporting on valuation concerns, the UNDP Sustainable Transport Infrastructure project page for Airports of Montenegro, the UNESCO State of Conservation report for Kotor (2009), the ScienceDirect publication on airport noise SEA for Tivat, Parkovi Dinarida documentation on Tivatska Solila, and the UNECE 4th Environmental Performance Review of Montenegro (2024).
key_concerns: Tivat Airport is immediately adjacent to Tivatska Solila, a Ramsar-listed wetland of international importance with 178 bird species and 21 endemic species, yet the concession process contains no publicly visible binding protections for this site

A 25-30 year concession designed to expand airport capacity will intensify tourism pressure on the UNESCO World Heritage Bay of Kotor, compounding existing problems of illegal construction and inadequate coastal wastewater infrastructure
The concession terms do not appear to include binding obligations aligned with EU ETS aviation requirements or decarbonization targets, creating potential obstacles for Chapter 27 closure
The most recent Strategic Environmental Assessment for Tivat Airport expansion dates to 2016 and is outdated; no new comprehensive SEA has been conducted for the multi-decade concession scenario
Spatial planning implications for the Montenegrin coastal zone from increased airport capacity and associated development have not been publicly assessed
The concession process follows a purely financial and political timeline with no evidence that environmental considerations are integrated into the decision-making process
recommendations: Require a new comprehensive Strategic Environmental Assessment before the concession proposal proceeds to Parliament, covering the full 25-30 year concession period and including cumulative impact assessment on Tivatska Solila and the Bay of Kotor

Embed binding environmental clauses in the concession agreement, including enforceable protections for the Tivatska Solila Ramsar site, noise and light pollution limits, and habitat disturbance mitigation measures
Include mandatory EU ETS alignment, Airport Carbon Accreditation targets, and decarbonization milestones in the concession terms, with penalties for non-compliance

Require the concessionaire to prepare and fund a Biodiversity Management Plan for the Tivatska Solila buffer zone, developed in consultation with the Environmental Protection Agency and Ramsar Convention authorities
Integrate the airport concession into the Spatial Plan of Montenegro until 2040, ensuring that capacity expansion plans are compatible with coastal zone management objectives
Establish an independent environmental monitoring body with authority to audit concessionaire compliance throughout the concession period, with reporting to Parliament

counter_proposal.title: Environmentally Conditioned Airport Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than advancing the concession on a purely financial timeline, the Ministry of Environment proposes conditioning parliamentary approval on the completion of a comprehensive Strategic Environmental Assessment and the inclusion of binding environmental clauses in the concession agreement. The concession should be structured as a green infrastructure deal that treats environmental performance as co-equal with financial performance, with specific protections for Tivatska Solila and the Bay of Kotor, mandatory EU ETS alignment, and independent environmental oversight throughout the 25-30 year term.
key_changes: Pause the parliamentary timeline until a new comprehensive SEA is completed covering the full concession period
Add a mandatory Annex on Environmental Obligations to the concession agreement, including specific protections for Tivatska Solila Ramsar site and UNESCO Bay of Kotor buffer zone
Require bidders to submit Environmental and Climate Action Plans as a scored component of their offers, weighted at minimum 20% of evaluation criteria
Include EU ETS compliance obligations and Airport Carbon Accreditation Level 3+ targets within the first 5 years of the concession
Establish a joint Environmental Monitoring Commission with representatives from the Environmental Protection Agency, civil society, and scientific institutions
Require the concessionaire to fund an annual Environmental Impact Fund of at least 2% of concession revenue for ecological restoration and monitoring
expected_benefits: Legal certainty that the concession is compatible with Ramsar Convention obligations and UNESCO World Heritage commitments
Stronger position in EU Chapter 27 negotiations by demonstrating environmental integration in major infrastructure decisions
Reduced long-term liability from environmental damage claims and potential Ramsar Convention compliance proceedings
Higher quality bids from international operators experienced in environmentally responsible airport management
Preserved biodiversity and ecosystem services at Tivatska Solila supporting sustainable coastal tourism
Climate-aligned infrastructure consistent with Montenegro's Paris Agreement commitments and EU Green Deal trajectory
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. Conducting a new SEA would require 6-9 months and may delay the concession timeline, but this is manageable given that the process has already been delayed since 2019. The IFC, as concession advisor, routinely incorporates environmental and social performance standards into its advisory work, so the institutional framework for embedding environmental clauses exists. The main obstacle is political will to treat environmental conditionality as non-negotiable rather than as a procedural formality. The cost of the SEA and monitoring framework is minimal compared to the concession value, estimated at several hundred thousand euros versus hundreds of millions in concession revenue.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir koncesije aerodroma uslovljen zaštitom životne sredine
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto napredovanja u postupku dodjele koncesije po isključivo finansijskom vremenskom planu, Ministarstvo ekologije predlaže da se parlamentarno odobrenje uslov i završetkom sveobuhvatne Strateške procjene uticaja na životnu sredinu i uključivanjem obavezujućih ekoloških klauzula u ugovor o koncesiji. Koncesija treba da bude strukturisana kao zeleni infrastrukturni aranžman koji ekološke performanse tretira ravnopravno s finansijskim, uz posebnu zaštitu Tivatske Solile i Bokokotorskog zaliva, obavezno usklađivanje sa EU ETS-om i nezavisni ekološki nadzor tokom čitavog perioda od 25 do 30 godina.
key_changes_mne: Pauzirati parlamentarni vremenski okvir do okončanja nove sveobuhvatne SPU koja pokriva cjelokupni period koncesije

Dodati obavezni Aneks o ekološkim obavezama uz ugovor o koncesiji, koji obuhvata posebnu zaštitu Ramsarskog lokaliteta Tivatska Solila i zaštitne zone UNESCO-vog Svjetskog naslijeđa – Bokokotorskog zaliva
Obavezati ponuđače da dostave Planove ekoloških i klimatskih mjera kao bodovanu komponentu ponude, s minimalnom težinom od 20% kriterijuma za ocjenjivanje
Uključiti obaveze usklađenosti sa EU ETS-om i ciljeve Akreditacije aerodroma za ugljenik nivoa 3+ u prvih 5 godina trajanja koncesije
Uspostaviti zajedničku Komisiju za ekološki monitoring s predstavnicima Agencije za zaštitu životne sredine, civilnog društva i naučnih institucija
Obavezati koncesionara da finansira godišnji Fond za zaštitu životne sredine u iznosu od najmanje 2% prihoda od koncesije, namijenjenog ekološkoj obnovi i monitoringu
expected_benefits_mne: Pravna sigurnost da je koncesija usklađena s obavezama iz Ramsarske konvencije i UNESCO-vim obavezama u pogledu Svjetskog naslijeđa
Jača pozicija u pregovorima o Poglavlju 27 s EU, demonstriranjem integracije zaštite životne sredine u važne infrastrukturne odluke
Smanjenje dugoročnih rizika od zahtjeva za naknadu ekološke štete i mogućih postupaka u vezi s usklađenošću s Ramsarskom konvencijom
Kvalitetnije ponude od međunarodnih operatera s iskustvom u ekološki odgovornom upravljanju aerodromima
Očuvana biodiverzitet i ekosistemske usluge na Tivatskoj Solili, koje podržavaju održivi obalni turizam
Klimatski usklađena infrastruktura konzistentna s obavezama Crne Gore iz Pariskog sporazuma i trajektorijom Evropskog zelenog dogovora
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Sprovođenje nove SPU zahtijevalo bi od 6 do 9 mjeseci i može odložiti vremenski plan koncesije, no to je upravivo uzimajući u obzir da je proces ionako odlagan od 2019. godine. IFC, kao savjetnik za koncesiju, rutinski uključuje standarde ekoloških i socijalnih performansi u svoj savjetodavni rad, pa institucionalni okvir za ugrađivanje ekoloških klauzula već postoji. Glavna prepreka je politička volja da se ekološka uslovljenost tretira kao nepregovarajući uslov, a ne kao proceduralna formalnost. Troškovi SPU i okvira za monitoring su minimalni u poređenju s vrijednošću koncesije – procjenjuju se na nekoliko stotina hiljada eura nasuprot stotinama miliona eura prihoda od koncesije.

assessments.summary_mne: Proces koncesije aerodroma koji napreduje ka parlamentarnoj razmatri pokreće značajna pitanja zaštite životne sredine koja su u velikoj mjeri zanemarena u aktuelnom okviru donošenja odluke. Aerodrom Tivat se nalazi neposredno uz Tivatsku Solilu, vlažno stanište od međunarodnog značaja uvršteno na Ramsarsku listu i jedno od posljednjih halofitnih staništa na istočnom Jadranu, dom 178 vrsta ptica i 21 endemske vrste. Koncesija od 25 do 30 godina s ciljevima proširenja i povećanja kapaciteta neizbježno će pojačati pritisak na ovo ekološki kritično područje, na UNESCO-om zaštićeno Bokokotorsko zaljevlje i na krhki crnogorski priobalni pojas. Vladino usredsređivanje na procjenu imovine i finansijske uslove, uz istovremeno zanemarivanje javnog definisanja obavezujućih mjera zaštite životne sredine u okviru ugovora o koncesiji, predstavlja propuštenu priliku i potencijalnu prijetnju usklađivanju s Poglavljem 27 EU. Bez snažnih ekoloških uslova, Crna Gora riskiruje da na decenijama učvrsti ekološki štetne operacije aerodroma, što je u suprotnosti s njenim putem ka pristupanju EU i klimatskim obavezama.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Proces koncesije aerodroma odvija se uz zabrinjavajući nedostatak vidljivih mjera zaštite životne sredine, uprkos blizini Aerodroma Tivat Ramsarskom lokalitetu Tivatska Solila i UNESCO-ovom Bokokotorskom zaljevlju. Ključna briga Ministarstva ekologije je da bi sporazum od 25 do 30 godina bez obavezujućih ekoloških klauzula mogao Crnu Goru da zaključa u proširenje infrastrukture neusklađene s zahtjevima EU Poglavlja 27 i klimatskim ciljevima. Preporučujemo zaustavljanje procesa koncesije do okončanja sveobuhvatne Strateške procjene uticaja na životnu sredinu i ugradnje primjenjivih ekoloških uslova u ugovor o koncesiji.
key_concerns_mne: Aerodrom Tivat se nalazi neposredno uz Tivatsku Solilu, vlažno stanište od međunarodnog značaja uvršteno na Ramsarsku listu, sa 178 vrsta ptica i 21 endemskom vrstom, a ipak proces koncesije ne sadrži javno vidljive obavezujuće zaštitne mjere za ovo područje

Koncesija od 25 do 30 godina osmišljena s ciljem proširenja kapaciteta aerodroma pojačaće turistički pritisak na Bokokotorsko zaljevlje, zaštićeno kao UNESCO-ova svjetska baština, čime se produbljuju postojeći problemi nelegalne gradnje i neadekvatne priobalne kanalizacione infrastrukture
Uslovi koncesije ne predviđaju obavezujuće obaveze usklađene s EU ETS zahtjevima za vazdušni saobraćaj ni s ciljevima dekarbonizacije, što može stvoriti prepreke za zatvaranje Poglavlja 27

Najnovija Strateška procjena uticaja na životnu sredinu za proširenje Aerodroma Tivat datira iz 2016. godine i zastarjela je; za scenarij višedecenijske koncesije nije sprovedena nova sveobuhvatna SPUOS
Implikacije prostornog planiranja za crnogorski priobalni pojas koje proistječu iz povećanog kapaciteta aerodroma i prateće izgradnje nisu javno procijenjene
Proces koncesije prati isključivo finansijski i politički vremenski okvir, bez ikakvih dokaza da su pitanja zaštite životne sredine integrisana u proces donošenja odluka
recommendations_mne: Zahtijevati sprovođenje nove sveobuhvatne Strateške procjene uticaja na životnu sredinu prije nego što prijedlog koncesije bude upućen Skupštini, koja bi obuhvatila cijeli period koncesije od 25 do 30 godina, uključujući procjenu kumulativnih uticaja na Tivatsku Solilu i Bokokotorsko zaljevlje

Ugraditi obavezujuće ekološke klauzule u ugovor o koncesiji, uključujući primjenjive mjere zaštite Ramsarskog lokaliteta Tivatska Solila, ograničenja buke i svjetlosnog zagađenja, te mjere ublažavanja narušavanja staništa
U uslove koncesije uvrstiti obavezno usklađivanje s EU ETS-om, ciljeve Akreditacije aerodroma za ugljik i prekretnice dekarbonizacije, uz predviđanje sankcija za nepoštovanje

Obavezati koncesionara na izradu i finansiranje Plana upravljanja biodiverzitetom za tampon-zonu Tivatske Solile, razvijenog u konsultaciji s Agencijom za zaštitu životne sredine i nadležnim organima Ramsarske konvencije
Integrisati koncesiju aerodroma u Prostorni plan Crne Gore do 2040. godine, osiguravajući da planovi za povećanje kapaciteta budu u skladu s ciljevima upravljanja priobalnim pojasom
Uspostaviti nezavisno tijelo za praćenje stanja životne sredine s ovlašćenjem za reviziju usklađenosti koncesionara tokom cijelog trajanja koncesije, uz obavezu izvještavanja Skupštini

assessments.summary: The airport concession process represents a potentially transformative opportunity for Montenegrin tourism infrastructure, but the Ministry of Tourism finds the current approach insufficiently anchored in tourism-sector safeguards, environmental protection commitments, and regional development obligations. A well-structured concession of Podgorica and Tivat airports could unlock badly needed terminal upgrades to handle projected growth from 3 million to over 5 million passengers, improve visitor experience, and expand route connectivity. However, the absence of binding environmental and carrying-capacity conditionalities in the concession framework, the lack of integration with northern region tourism development (particularly the stalled Berane airport), and the 25-30 year lock-in without clear sustainability benchmarks pose serious risks to Montenegro's tourism quality, coastal zone integrity, and EU Chapter 27 alignment.
assessments.executive_summary: The airport concession is a positive step for tourism infrastructure modernization but scores only 6/10 due to critical gaps: no binding environmental or carrying-capacity conditions for Tivat (adjacent to the UNESCO-listed Kotor Bay), no linkage to northern region airport development, and a 25-30 year commitment without sustainability benchmarks aligned with EU Chapter 27. The Ministry recommends pausing the process until environmental impact assessments and tourism carrying-capacity thresholds are embedded as enforceable concession obligations.
assessments.reasoning:
INFRASTRUCTURE NEED IS REAL AND URGENT: Montenegro's airports handled over 3 million passengers in 2025, up from 850,000 when terminals were last significantly updated in 2006. Tivat Airport alone served over 1.3 million passengers in 2025, with 80% concentrated in the May-September peak season. The current terminal infrastructure is strained and directly degrades the tourist arrival experience. A 25% projected increase in Tivat passenger traffic for 2026 underscores the urgency. The concession, if properly structured, could deliver the proposed expansion to 3 million annual capacity at Tivat alone by 2030, which would be transformative for Montenegro's tourism competitiveness versus Croatia, Albania, and Greece.

VALUATION AND PARLIAMENTARY OVERSIGHT ARE APPROPRIATE: The government's decision to seek a fresh valuation before proceeding is procedurally sound. The 2018 estimate of EUR 140 million and the 2021 fixed-asset valuation of EUR 122 million are both outdated given passenger growth. If the new valuation exceeds EUR 150 million (which is highly likely given traffic trends), Parliamentary approval will be required, adding democratic legitimacy. The union's call for independent, multi-assessor valuation is reasonable and should be heeded.

CRITICAL GAP: ENVIRONMENTAL AND COASTAL ZONE PROTECTIONS: Tivat Airport's runway extends to within 88 meters of the Adriatic coastline, immediately adjacent to Kotor Bay, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Expanding airport capacity without binding environmental conditionalities creates serious risks. Bird strikes have already increased from 26 (2023) to 38 (2024), reflecting wildlife-habitat pressure. Academic research has documented airport noise impacts requiring Strategic Environmental Assessment for Tivat expansion plans. UNESCO has specifically called for SEAs for any plans affecting the Kotor World Heritage property. The concession framework, as publicly reported, does not appear to include enforceable environmental performance benchmarks, noise abatement investment obligations, or coastal zone protection conditions. This is a significant omission.

CARRYING CAPACITY AND OVERTOURISM: Kotor already faces severe overtourism pressure, with approximately 500 cruise ship calls annually bringing over 500,000 passengers to a town of 23,000 residents. Expanding Tivat Airport capacity to 3 million passengers without integrated carrying-capacity management for the Boka Bay region risks compounding this problem. The concession should include mandatory coordination with municipal tourism management plans and visitor caps for the Kotor UNESCO zone. The GSTC Destination Assessment conducted in 2023-2024 should inform binding concession conditions.

NORTHERN REGION DEVELOPMENT NEGLECTED: The concession covers only Podgorica and Tivat, entrenching the coast-centric model that perpetuates economic disparity between Montenegro's north and south. The Spatial Plan of Montenegro until 2040 envisions seven airports including Berane, but the Berane airport revitalization (EUR 75 million, led by Elite Private Jet Service) is stalled with no construction started. By locking in a 25-30 year concession for the two main airports without linking it to northern connectivity investment, the government misses an opportunity to use concession revenues to fund Berane and other northern infrastructure that would diversify tourism beyond the overcrowded coast.

EU CHAPTER 27 IMPLICATIONS: Montenegro's EU accession progress on Chapter 27 remains at "a certain level of preparedness" with only "limited progress" on the Action Plan for closing benchmarks. The European Commission has flagged slow implementation, pending nature protection legislation, and the absence of a National Energy and Climate Plan. A major infrastructure concession of this magnitude should demonstrably advance Chapter 27 compliance, particularly regarding: Environmental Impact Assessment requirements (EIA Directive), Strategic Environmental Assessment for the spatial planning changes needed for expansion, noise and air quality monitoring aligned with EU directives, and climate change mitigation commitments. The concession contract should require the concessionaire to meet EU environmental standards from day one, not just Montenegrin standards, thereby using the private investment as an accelerator for Chapter 27 alignment.

BIDDER CONTROVERSY RAISES PROCESS INTEGRITY CONCERNS: The scoring discrepancies between evaluation rounds (IIAC initially scored 79.7, below the 80-point threshold, then was re-scored to 81.69) and CAAP's announced intention to appeal or pursue international arbitration introduce legal and reputational risk. From a tourism perspective, prolonged legal disputes could delay airport upgrades by years, damaging Montenegro's competitive position. The government should ensure absolute process transparency to withstand legal challenge.

CONCESSION REVENUE ALLOCATION: Reports indicate 75% of concession revenues would go to municipalities for strategic projects. The Ministry of Tourism notes that this allocation should be ring-fenced for tourism-related infrastructure, environmental protection, and carrying-capacity management rather than general municipal spending. Without earmarking, coastal municipalities may use funds for construction that worsens rather than manages tourism pressure.
key_concerns: No binding environmental impact assessment or Strategic Environmental Assessment requirements embedded in the concession contract for Tivat Airport expansion, despite its location 88 meters from the Adriatic coast and adjacent to the UNESCO-listed Kotor Bay

Expanding Tivat capacity to 3 million passengers without integrated carrying-capacity management risks worsening overtourism in Kotor and the Boka Bay region, which already receives over 500,000 cruise passengers annually in a town of 23,000

The 25-30 year concession for Podgorica and Tivat excludes northern airports like Berane, entrenching coast-centric tourism and perpetuating the economic disparity between Montenegro's north and south

Scoring irregularities in the tender process (IIAC initially below the 80-point threshold, then re-scored above it) and CAAP's threatened international arbitration could delay airport upgrades and damage Montenegro's investment reputation

No apparent requirement for the concessionaire to meet EU environmental standards (noise, air quality, waste management) aligned with Chapter 27 benchmarks, missing an opportunity to use private investment to accelerate EU accession compliance

Concession revenue allocation to municipalities (75%) lacks earmarking for tourism sustainability and environmental protection, risking misallocation toward construction that worsens coastal zone pressures

The outdated asset valuation (last comprehensive assessment in 2021 at EUR 122 million) may result in undervaluation given 2025 traffic exceeding 3 million passengers, potentially shortchanging public interest
recommendations: Embed mandatory Environmental Impact Assessment and Strategic Environmental Assessment requirements in the concession contract, specifically for any Tivat Airport expansion, in compliance with the EU EIA Directive and UNESCO requirements for the Kotor Bay World Heritage Site

Introduce binding tourism carrying-capacity thresholds in the concession agreement that link Tivat passenger growth to municipal tourism management plans and Kotor visitor caps, informed by the 2023-2024 GSTC Destination Assessment

Require the concessionaire to meet EU-level environmental standards for noise, air quality, waste management, and energy efficiency from the start of operations, using the concession as a Chapter 27 compliance accelerator

Link concession revenue allocation (the reported 75% municipal share) to earmarked spending on tourism sustainability, coastal zone management, and environmental protection through legally binding revenue-sharing conditions

Integrate northern region airport development (particularly Berane) into the concession framework, either by requiring the concessionaire to invest in or provide technical assistance to northern airports, or by earmarking a percentage of concession revenues for northern connectivity

Ensure process transparency by publishing all scoring methodologies, evaluation reports, and the full draft concession contract before Parliamentary review, to withstand CAAP's legal challenge and maintain investor confidence

Commission the fresh valuation from multiple independent assessors as the union demands, ensuring it accounts for current traffic volumes (3+ million passengers), projected growth, and the strategic value of Tivat as the primary gateway to the Adriatic coast

Include a mid-term review clause (at year 10 and year 20) in the 25-30 year concession allowing renegotiation of environmental and capacity terms based on updated EU standards and climate change projections

counter_proposal.title: Tourism-Anchored Green Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than proceeding with the current concession structure that treats airports purely as transport infrastructure, the Ministry of Tourism proposes a Green Concession Framework that embeds tourism sustainability, environmental protection, and regional equity as core contractual obligations. This framework would make the concession a vehicle for EU Chapter 27 compliance, UNESCO World Heritage protection, and northern region development rather than simply a revenue and infrastructure deal.
key_changes: Add a mandatory Tourism and Environment Annex to the concession contract with enforceable KPIs: annual noise monitoring, air quality reporting, waste recycling rates, and carbon emissions tracking aligned with EU standards

Establish a Tivat Carrying Capacity Protocol linking airport slot allocation to real-time visitor density data in Kotor Bay, with seasonal caps during peak months and mandatory coordination with cruise ship scheduling

Earmark 20% of the annual concession fee specifically for a Northern Tourism Connectivity Fund to finance Berane airport development, mountain resort access roads, and national park visitor infrastructure
Require the concessionaire to complete a full Strategic Environmental Assessment before any terminal expansion at Tivat, with UNESCO World Heritage Centre consultation as a contractual obligation
Institute 10-year and 20-year review gates in the concession allowing renegotiation of environmental standards and capacity limits to reflect evolving EU directives and climate science

Mandate that the concessionaire develop at least 5 new year-round routes to European secondary cities to reduce seasonality, with route development subsidies funded from the concession agreement rather than public budgets
expected_benefits: Transforms the airport concession from a pure infrastructure deal into a strategic instrument for sustainable tourism development and EU accession
Protects UNESCO World Heritage status of Kotor Bay by creating a legally binding link between airport growth and destination carrying capacity
Accelerates Chapter 27 compliance by requiring EU-standard environmental monitoring and reporting from a major concessionaire from day one
Addresses the north-south economic divide by channeling concession revenues into northern tourism connectivity, supporting diversification beyond the coast
Reduces tourism seasonality through mandated year-round route development, improving airport utilization and spreading economic benefits across more months
Creates a replicable model for other Montenegrin infrastructure concessions that balances investor returns with public interest and environmental protection
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. The key changes are contractual additions rather than structural redesigns, meaning they could be incorporated into the existing tender framework before final offers are due on May 9. The Northern Tourism Connectivity Fund would require legislative action but could be structured as a concession revenue condition. The main risk is that adding environmental and capacity conditions may reduce the financial attractiveness to bidders, potentially lowering the upfront payment. However, international precedent (e.g., London Gatwick, Athens International) shows that concessionaires accept sustainability obligations when they are clearly defined upfront. The 10-year review gates add flexibility that actually reduces long-term risk for both parties.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Zeleni okvir koncesija usmjeren na turizam
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto nastavka sa trenutnom strukturom koncesije koja aerodrome tretira isključivo kao transportnu infrastrukturu, Ministarstvo turizma predlaže Zeleni okvir koncesija koji održivost turizma, zaštitu životne sredine i regionalnu ravnopravnost ugrađuje kao osnovne ugovorne obaveze. Ovaj okvir bi pretvorio koncesiju u instrument za usklađivanje sa Poglavljem 27 EU, zaštitu UNESCO-vog svjetskog naslijeđa i razvoj sjeverne regije, a ne samo u dogovor o prihodima i infrastrukturi.
key_changes_mne: Dodati obavezan Aneks o turizmu i životnoj sredini uz ugovor o koncesiji sa izvršivim KPI-jevima: godišnji monitoring buke, izvještavanje o kvalitetu vazduha, stope reciklaže otpada i praćenje emisija ugljika usklađeno sa standardima EU

Uspostaviti Protokol o prihvatnom kapacitetu Tivta koji povezuje dodjelu aerodromskih slotova sa podacima o gustini posjetilaca u Bokokotorskom zalivu u realnom vremenu, sa sezonskim ograničenjima tokom vršnih mjeseci i obaveznom koordinacijom sa rasporedom kruzera

Namijeniti 20% godišnje naknade za koncesiju isključivo za Fond za turističku povezanost sjevera radi finansiranja razvoja aerodroma u Beranama, pristupnih puteva planinskim odmaralištima i infrastrukture za posjetioce nacionalnih parkova

Obavezati koncesionara da izvrši potpunu Stratešku procjenu uticaja na životnu sredinu prije bilo kakvog proširenja terminala u Tivtu, uz konsultacije sa UNESCO-vim Centrom za svjetsku baštinu kao ugovornom obavezom

Uvesti kontrolne tačke revizije na 10 i 20 godina u okviru koncesije koje omogućavaju ponovni pregled ekoloških standarda i ograničenja kapaciteta radi usklađivanja sa izmijenjenim direktivama EU i klimatskim naučnim spoznajama

Obavezati koncesionara da razvije najmanje 5 novih cjelogodišnjih linija ka sekundarnim evropskim gradovima radi smanjenja sezonalnosti, pri čemu se subvencije za razvoj linija finansiraju iz ugovora o koncesiji, a ne iz javnih budžeta
expected_benefits_mne: Pretvara aerodromsku koncesiju iz čisto infrastrukturnog dogovora u strateški instrument za održivi razvoj turizma i pristupanje EU
Štiti status UNESCO-vog svjetskog naslijeđa Bokokotorskog zaliva stvaranjem pravno obavezujuće veze između rasta aerodroma i prihvatnog kapaciteta destinacije
Ubrzava usklađivanje sa Poglavljem 27 zahtijevanjem praćenja životne sredine i izvještavanja prema standardima EU od strane velikog koncesionara od prvog dana
Bavi se ekonomskom podjelom između sjevera i juga usmjeravanjem prihoda od koncesije u turističku povezanost sjevera, podržavajući diversifikaciju izvan primorja
Smanjuje sezonalnost turizma kroz obavezni razvoj cjelogodišnjih linija, poboljšavajući iskorišćenost aerodroma i šireći ekonomske koristi na više mjeseci u godini
Stvara prenosivi model za ostale koncesije infrastrukture u Crnoj Gori koji usklađuje prinose investitora sa javnim interesom i zaštitom životne sredine
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Ključne izmjene predstavljaju ugovorne dopune, a ne strukturalna redizajniranja, što znači da bi mogle biti uvrštene u postojeći okvir tendera prije isteka roka za finalne ponude 9. maja. Fond za turističku povezanost sjevera zahtijevao bi zakonodavne mjere, ali bi mogao biti strukturiran kao uslov prihoda od koncesije. Glavni rizik je da dodavanje ekoloških uslova i uslova kapaciteta može umanjiti finansijsku atraktivnost za ponuđače, potencijalno smanjujući avansnu uplatu. Međutim, međunarodni presedani (npr. London Gatvik, Atinska međunarodna luka) pokazuju da koncesionari prihvataju obaveze u oblasti održivosti kada su jasno definisane unaprijed. Kontrolne tačke revizije na 10 godina dodaju fleksibilnost koja zapravo smanjuje dugoročni rizik za obje strane.

assessments.summary_mne: Proces koncesije aerodroma predstavlja potencijalno transformativnu priliku za turističku infrastrukturu Crne Gore, ali Ministarstvo turizma smatra da trenutni pristup nije dovoljno utemeljen u zaštitnim mjerama turističkog sektora, obavezama zaštite životne sredine i obavezama regionalnog razvoja. Dobro strukturirana koncesija aerodroma Podgorica i Tivat mogla bi otključati prijeko potrebne nadogradnje terminala radi prihvatanja predviđenog rasta s 3 miliona na više od 5 miliona putnika, poboljšati iskustvo posjetilaca i proširiti mrežu destinacija. Međutim, odsustvo obavezujućih uslova zaštite životne sredine i nosivog kapaciteta u okviru koncesije, nedostatak integracije s razvojem turizma u sjevernoj regiji (posebno u pogledu zapustelog aerodroma Berane) te zaključanost na period od 25 do 30 godina bez jasnih mjerila održivosti predstavljaju ozbiljne rizike po kvalitet crnogorskog turizma, integritet priobalnog pojasa i usklađenost s Poglavljem 27 EU.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Koncesija aerodroma je pozitivan korak u modernizaciji turističke infrastrukture, ali dobija ocjenu samo 6/10 zbog kritičnih nedostataka: nema obavezujućih ekoloških uslova ni uslova nosivog kapaciteta za Tivat (koji se graniči s Kotorskim zalivom uvrštenim na UNESCO-vu listu), nema veze s razvojem aerodroma u sjevernoj regiji, a radi se o obavezi na 25 do 30 godina bez mjerila održivosti usklađenih s Poglavljem 27 EU. Ministarstvo preporučuje obustavu procesa dok procjene uticaja na životnu sredinu i pragovi nosivog kapaciteta turizma ne budu ugrađeni kao izvršive obaveze iz koncesije.
key_concerns_mne: Ne postoje obavezujući zahtjevi za procjenu uticaja na životnu sredinu niti za Stratešku procjenu uticaja na životnu sredinu ugrađeni u ugovor o koncesiji za proširenje Aerodroma Tivat, uprkos tome što se nalazi na svega 88 metara od obale Jadranskog mora i u neposrednoj blizini Kotorskog zaliva koji je na UNESCO-voj listi svjetske baštine

Povećanje kapaciteta Tivta na 3 miliona putnika bez integrisanog upravljanja nosivim kapacitetom prijeti pogoršanjem prekomjernog turizma u Kotoru i oblasti Boke kotorske, koja već prima više od 500.000 kruzera godišnje u gradu s 23.000 stanovnika

Koncesija od 25 do 30 godina za aerodrome Podgorica i Tivat isključuje sjeverne aerodrome poput Berana, čime se učvršćuje turizam usmjeren na primorje i produbljuje ekonomski jaz između sjevera i juga Crne Gore

Nepravilnosti u bodovanju tokom tenderskog procesa (IIAC je prvobitno bio ispod praga od 80 bodova, a potom je prepuno bodovan iznad njega) i prijetnja CAAP-a međunarodnom arbitražom mogle bi odložiti modernizaciju aerodroma i narušiti reputaciju Crne Gore kao destinacije za investicije

Ne postoji vidljiv zahtjev da korisnik koncesije ispunjava standarde EU za zaštitu životne sredine (buka, kvalitet vazduha, upravljanje otpadom) usklađene s mjerilima Poglavlja 27, čime se propušta prilika da se privatna investicija iskoristi za ubrzanje ispunjavanja uslova pristupanja EU

Raspodjela prihoda od koncesije opštinama (75%) ne predviđa namjensko trošenje za održivost turizma i zaštitu životne sredine, što otvara rizik od pogrešne upotrebe sredstava za gradnju koja bi dodatno opteretila priobalni pojas

Zastarjela procjena imovine (posljednja sveobuhvatna procjena iz 2021. godine na 122 miliona EUR) može dovesti do potcjenjivanja vrijednosti s obzirom na to da je promet 2025. premašio 3 miliona putnika, čime bi se nanijela šteta javnom interesu
recommendations_mne: Ugraditi obavezne zahtjeve za Procjenu uticaja na životnu sredinu i Stratešku procjenu uticaja na životnu sredinu u ugovor o koncesiji, posebno za svako proširenje Aerodroma Tivat, u skladu s Direktivom EU o procjeni uticaja na životnu sredinu i zahtjevima UNESCO-a za lokalitet svjetske baštine Kotorski zaliv

Uvesti obavezujuće pragove nosivog kapaciteta turizma u ugovor o koncesiji koji će rast broja putnika u Tivtu usloviti opštinskim planovima upravljanja turizmom i ograničenjem broja posjetilaca Kotora, u skladu s GSTC procjenom destinacije iz perioda 2023–2024

Obavezati korisnika koncesije da od samog početka poslovanja ispunjava standarde EU na nivou zaštite životne sredine u pogledu buke, kvaliteta vazduha, upravljanja otpadom i energetske efikasnosti, koristeći koncesiju kao pokretač usklađivanja s Poglavljem 27

Raspodjelu prihoda od koncesije (prijavljeni udio opština od 75%) vezati za namjensku potrošnju na održivost turizma, upravljanje priobalnim pojasom i zaštitu životne sredine putem pravno obavezujućih uslova raspodjele prihoda

Integrisati razvoj aerodroma u sjevernoj regiji (posebno Berana) u okvir koncesije, bilo obavezivanjem korisnika koncesije da investira ili pruža tehničku pomoć sjevernim aerodromima, ili namjenskim izdvajanjem dijela prihoda od koncesije za poboljšanje saobraćajne povezanosti sjevera

Osigurati transparentnost procesa objavljivanjem svih metodologija bodovanja, izvještaja o ocjenjivanju i potpunog nacrta ugovora o koncesiji prije parlamentarne rasprave, radi izdržavanja pravnog izazova CAAP-a i očuvanja povjerenja investitora

Naručiti novu procjenu vrijednosti od više nezavisnih procjenitelja, kako traži sindikat, koja će uzeti u obzir trenutne obime saobraćaja (3+ miliona putnika), projektovani rast i stratešku vrijednost Tivta kao glavne kapije prema Jadranskom moru

Uključiti klauzulu o preispitivanju na sredini roka (u 10. i 20. godini) u koncesiju od 25 do 30 godina, kojom se omogućava renegocijacija ekoloških uslova i uslova kapaciteta na osnovu ažuriranih standarda EU i projekcija klimatskih promjena

assessments.summary: The airport concession process carries significant implications for the Ministry of Interior across border management, civil protection, and organized crime prevention. While the decision to await a fresh valuation is procedurally sound and legally required under the Law on State Property, the concession framework as publicly discussed lacks sufficient detail on how critical interior security functions -- border police operations, emergency rescue services, Schengen-readiness infrastructure, and anti-corruption safeguards -- will be contractually protected under a 25-30 year private concession. The Ministry finds the process neither clearly harmful nor clearly beneficial from a security standpoint, but notes that without explicit, enforceable security provisions in the concession agreement, Montenegro risks undermining its EU accession trajectory on Chapters 23 and 24.
assessments.executive_summary: The airport concession process is procedurally reasonable but raises serious unaddressed security concerns for the Ministry of Interior. The concession agreement must explicitly guarantee state authority over border police operations, Schengen-compliant infrastructure upgrades, and civil protection functions at both airports. Without binding security clauses and robust anti-corruption oversight of the concession process itself, this deal risks compromising Montenegro's Chapter 24 progress and creating long-term vulnerabilities in border management and organized crime prevention.
assessments.reasoning:
From the Ministry of Interior's perspective, the concession of Podgorica and Tivat airports for 25-30 years represents a major structural change to two of Montenegro's most security-sensitive national infrastructure assets. The decision to await a new valuation is legally required and procedurally appropriate -- the 2018 estimate of 140 million euros is outdated and may fall below or above the 150 million euro parliamentary threshold under the Law on State Property. However, the valuation question is only one dimension of a far more complex security equation.

Border Management and Schengen Readiness: Podgorica and Tivat airports are Montenegro's primary international air entry points. Border police stations at both airports are operated by the Police Directorate (Uprava policije), and passport control, immigration, and customs enforcement remain sovereign state functions. However, a private concessionaire controlling airport infrastructure for three decades will have decisive influence over the physical layout, passenger flow design, technology systems, and space allocation that directly affect border police operational effectiveness. The concession agreement must contain enforceable provisions guaranteeing adequate space, technology integration points, and infrastructure standards for border police operations. This is especially critical given Montenegro's ambition to close all EU accession chapters by the end of 2026, with Chapters 23 and 24 (Justice, Freedom and Security) being the most demanding. The European Commission's 2025 Enlargement Package noted Montenegro's positive Interim Benchmark Assessment Report for Chapter 24, but also flagged that legislative alignment slowed in early 2025. Any concession arrangement that impedes Schengen-standard border infrastructure upgrades could jeopardize this progress. Montenegro currently holds EU One Stop Security recognition for its aviation security standards -- a status that must be contractually protected.

Civil Protection and Emergency Services: Under the Law on Protection and Rescue, the Ministry of Interior's Rescue and Protection Directorate is responsible for emergency management at the national level. Airports require ICAO-compliant Rescue and Fire Fighting Services (ARFF) as mandatory under Annex 14. The concession agreement must clearly delineate responsibilities for airport emergency response, including who funds, staffs, and maintains ARFF capabilities. A 25-30 year concession creates risk that cost-optimization pressures could erode emergency response standards if obligations are not precisely specified and regularly audited. The split between concessionaire responsibilities (typically landside operations) and state responsibilities (airside security, border control) must be unambiguous, with clear protocols for coordination during emergencies, terrorist incidents, or civil protection activations.

Organized Crime and Corruption Risks: Montenegro's organized crime environment presents material risks to any major concession process. The Organized Crime Index and MONEYVAL assessments document state-embedded actors maintaining connections to criminal networks, corruption within law enforcement and customs, and exploitation of transportation infrastructure for smuggling. The Port of Bar has been a documented hub for cigarette and cocaine smuggling, and airports are similarly vulnerable nodes. The concession process itself must be shielded from corrupt influence -- the involvement of IFC as advisor provides some procedural safeguard, but the Ministry notes that the Airports of Montenegro Syndicate has called for independent, multiple, and publicly verifiable valuations, suggesting insufficient transparency to date. With three bidders submitting offers by May 9, the integrity of the evaluation and selection process is paramount. Any concessionaire must undergo rigorous beneficial ownership screening, and the agreement should include anti-money-laundering compliance obligations and provisions for state access to airport operational data relevant to law enforcement.

Administrative Capacity: The Ministry is concerned about the state's capacity to effectively oversee a complex 25-30 year concession agreement. Montenegro's public administration has historically struggled with regulatory oversight of concessions -- as documented in other sectors where concessionaire abuses went unchecked. The Ministry of Transport, which leads this process, must coordinate closely with Interior on security provisions, but there is no public indication that a systematic security impact assessment has been conducted or that Interior has been formally consulted on the concession agreement terms.

The parliamentary review threshold is a positive democratic safeguard, and the Ministry supports ensuring that any agreement exceeding 150 million euros receives full parliamentary scrutiny. However, parliamentary review alone is insufficient without a dedicated security annex to the concession agreement that has been developed with Interior Ministry input.
key_concerns: No public evidence that the Ministry of Interior has been formally consulted on security provisions of the concession agreement, despite airports being critical border management infrastructure

The 25-30 year concession duration gives a private operator decisive control over physical infrastructure that directly affects border police operational effectiveness, passenger screening, and immigration enforcement
Montenegro's Chapter 24 EU accession progress could be jeopardized if the concession agreement does not mandate Schengen-compliant border infrastructure upgrades and maintenance standards
The concession process itself is vulnerable to organized crime influence and corruption, given Montenegro's documented patterns of state-embedded criminal actors exploiting infrastructure deals
Unclear delineation of civil protection and emergency response responsibilities between the concessionaire and the state's Rescue and Protection Directorate
Insufficient transparency in the valuation process, as flagged by the Airports of Montenegro Syndicate, which increases corruption risk
No public framework for how the concessionaire will be required to share operational data with law enforcement for counter-narcotics, counter-terrorism, and border security purposes
Risk that cost-optimization by a private concessionaire could degrade ICAO-mandated Rescue and Fire Fighting Service standards over the concession period
recommendations: Mandate a formal Security Impact Assessment of the concession agreement, led jointly by the Ministry of Interior and Police Directorate, before the proposal reaches cabinet or Parliament

Include a binding Security Annex in the concession agreement that guarantees state authority over border police operations, specifies minimum infrastructure standards for border control facilities, and requires concessionaire cooperation with Schengen-readiness upgrades

Require rigorous beneficial ownership screening and anti-money-laundering due diligence on all three bidders, conducted by or in coordination with the Administration for Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing

Establish a dedicated inter-ministerial security oversight committee (Interior, Transport, Defence) with contractual authority to audit and enforce security provisions throughout the 25-30 year concession

Insert contractual obligations for the concessionaire to share operational data (CCTV, passenger manifests, cargo records) with law enforcement agencies upon request, in compliance with data protection law
Specify minimum ARFF staffing, equipment, and response-time standards in the concession agreement, with penalties for non-compliance and regular audits by the Rescue and Protection Directorate
Ensure the concession agreement includes break clauses or penalty mechanisms triggered by security standard breaches, including failure to maintain EU One Stop Security recognition
Request that Parliament, if the deal exceeds the 150 million euro threshold, receive a dedicated security briefing from the Ministry of Interior before voting on the concession

counter_proposal.title: Security-First Airport Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Interior proposes that the concession process be paused until a comprehensive Security Impact Assessment is completed and a binding Security Annex is drafted as an integral part of the concession agreement. Rather than treating security as an afterthought to a primarily financial transaction, Interior would embed security requirements from the outset, ensuring that border management, civil protection, and anti-corruption safeguards are non-negotiable conditions of any concession award. The Ministry would also require that all three bidders undergo enhanced security vetting beyond standard commercial due diligence.
key_changes: Conduct a formal Security Impact Assessment before the concession proposal proceeds to cabinet, covering border management, civil protection, counter-terrorism, and organized crime risks
Draft a mandatory Security Annex to the concession agreement specifying border police infrastructure standards, data-sharing obligations, ARFF requirements, and Schengen upgrade commitments
Require enhanced security vetting of all bidders, including beneficial ownership transparency, anti-money-laundering checks, and screening against organized crime databases (SELEC, Europol, Interpol)
Establish a permanent inter-ministerial Airport Security Oversight Board with contractual enforcement powers throughout the concession period
Include mandatory five-year security review clauses allowing the state to renegotiate security provisions as EU accession requirements evolve
expected_benefits: Ensures border police operational effectiveness is contractually protected for the full concession duration
Safeguards Montenegro's EU One Stop Security status and supports Schengen-readiness preparations aligned with Chapter 24 benchmarks
Reduces corruption risk in the concession award process through enhanced bidder vetting
Creates a durable institutional mechanism for security oversight that outlasts individual governments
Provides legal basis for state intervention if security standards degrade under private management
Strengthens Montenegro's negotiating position on Chapters 23 and 24 by demonstrating proactive security governance of critical infrastructure
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. A Security Impact Assessment can be completed within 60-90 days using existing Ministry of Interior and Police Directorate capacity, supplemented by Frontex advisory support if needed. The Security Annex can be drafted in parallel with the ongoing valuation process without delaying the May 9 bid deadline. Enhanced bidder vetting can leverage existing cooperation frameworks with Europol, SELEC, and national financial intelligence units. The inter-ministerial oversight board can be established by government decree without new legislation. The primary risk is political resistance from the Ministry of Transport or bidders who may view additional security requirements as burdensome, but this is outweighed by the reputational and accession benefits of demonstrating security-first governance.

assessments.summary_mne: Proces koncesije aerodroma nosi značajne implikacije za Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova u pogledu upravljanja granicama, civilne zaštite i sprečavanja organizovanog kriminala. Iako je odluka da se sačeka nova procjena vrijednosti proceduralno opravdana i zakonski obavezna u skladu sa Zakonom o državnoj imovini, koncesijski okvir, onako kako je javno predstavljen, ne sadrži dovoljno detalja o tome kako će kritične funkcije unutrašnje bezbjednosti — operacije granične policije, službe hitnog spasavanja, infrastruktura za šengensku usklađenost i zaštitni mehanizmi protiv korupcije — biti ugovorno zaštićene u okviru privatne koncesije na 25–30 godina. Ministarstvo smatra da proces nije ni jasno štetan ni jasno koristan sa stanovišta bezbjednosti, ali napominje da bez izričitih i izvršivih odredbi o bezbjednosti u ugovoru o koncesiji, Crna Gora risikuje da ugrozi svoju trajektoriju pristupanja EU u okviru Poglavlja 23 i 24.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Proces dodjele koncesije aerodroma proceduralno je razuman, ali otvara ozbiljna i do sada nerazriješena bezbjednosna pitanja od značaja za Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova. Ugovorom o koncesiji mora se izričito garantovati državni autoritet nad operacijama granične policije, unapređenjima infrastrukture u skladu sa šengenskim standardima i funkcijama civilne zaštite na oba aerodroma. Bez obavezujućih bezbjednosnih klauzula i snažnog mehanizma nadzora nad samim procesom dodjele koncesije radi suzbijanja korupcije, ovaj posao risikuje da ugrozi napredak Crne Gore u okviru Poglavlja 24 i da stvori dugoročne ranjivosti u oblasti upravljanja granicama i sprečavanja organizovanog kriminala.
key_concerns_mne: Ne postoje javni dokazi da je Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova formalno konsultovano o bezbjednosnim odredbama ugovora o koncesiji, uprkos tome što su aerodromi kritična infrastruktura za upravljanje granicama

Trajanje koncesije od 25–30 godina daje privatnom operateru odlučujuću kontrolu nad fizičkom infrastrukturom koja direktno utiče na operativnu efikasnost granične policije, pregled putnika i sprovođenje imigracijskih propisa

Napredak Crne Gore u okviru Poglavlja 24 procesa pristupanja EU mogao bi biti doveden u pitanje ukoliko ugovorom o koncesiji ne budu propisane nadogradnje granične infrastrukture u skladu sa šengenskim standardima, kao i odgovarajući standardi održavanja

Sam proces dodjele koncesije podložan je uticaju organizovanog kriminala i korupcije, s obzirom na dokumentovane obrasce u kojima kriminalni akteri ukorijenjeni u državnim strukturama eksploatišu infrastrukturne poslove u Crnoj Gori
Nejasna razgraničenost nadležnosti između koncesionara i državne Uprave za zaštitu i spašavanje u oblasti civilne zaštite i odgovora na vanredne situacije
Nedovoljna transparentnost u procesu procjene vrijednosti, na što je ukazao Sindikat aerodroma Crne Gore, što povećava rizik od korupcije

Ne postoji javni okvir kojim bi se uredila obaveza koncesionara da s organima za sprovođenje zakona dijeli operativne podatke u svrhe suzbijanja narkotika, borbe protiv terorizma i bezbjednosti granica
Rizik da bi optimizacija troškova od strane privatnog koncesionara mogla tokom trajanja koncesije umanjiti standarde Službe za spasavanje i gašenje požara propisane od strane ICAO-a
recommendations_mne: Obavezati nadležne organe da sprovedu formalnu Procjenu uticaja na bezbjednost ugovora o koncesiji, kojom zajednički rukovode Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova i Uprava policije, prije nego što prijedlog dospije pred Vladu ili Skupštinu

Uključiti obavezujući Bezbjednosni aneks u ugovor o koncesiji kojim se garantuje državni autoritet nad operacijama granične policije, utvrđuju minimalni infrastrukturni standardi za objekte granične kontrole i nalaže saradnja koncesionara na sprovođenju unapređenja usklađenih sa šengenskim zahtjevima

Zahtijevati rigoroznu provjeru stvarnih vlasnika i dubinsku analizu u skladu s propisima o sprečavanju pranja novca za sva tri ponuđača, koju sprovodi ili koordinira Uprava za sprečavanje pranja novca i finansiranja terorizma

Uspostaviti poseban međuresorski odbor za nadzor bezbjednosti (unutrašnji poslovi, saobraćaj, odbrana) s ugovornim ovlašćenjem za reviziju i sprovođenje bezbjednosnih odredbi tokom čitavog trajanja koncesije od 25–30 godina

Ugovorom propisati obaveze koncesionara da organima za sprovođenje zakona, na zahtjev i u skladu s propisima o zaštiti podataka, dostavlja operativne podatke (snimke video-nadzora, liste putnika, evidencije tereta)

Ugovorom o koncesiji precizirati minimalne standarde u pogledu broja osoblja ARFF-a, opreme i vremena odgovora, uz propisivanje kazni za nepoštovanje i redovne revizije od strane Uprave za zaštitu i spašavanje

Osigurati da ugovor o koncesiji sadrži klauzule o raskidu ili mehanizme sankcija koji se aktiviraju u slučaju kršenja bezbjednosnih standarda, uključujući propust u održavanju priznatog statusa u okviru EU koncepta jednokratne bezbjednosne kontrole (One Stop Security)
Zatražiti da Skupština, ukoliko vrijednost posla prelazi prag od 150 miliona eura, prije glasanja o koncesiji primi poseban bezbjednosni izvještaj od Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova

assessments.summary: The decision to commission a fresh asset valuation of Airports of Montenegro is a procedurally necessary step, but it arrives far too late in a concession process that has already been severely compromised by documented irregularities, Tender Commission resignations, IFC withdrawal threats, and credible allegations of undue influence on evaluators. The valuation exercise risks becoming a procedural formality rather than a genuine safeguard, particularly given that the 2018 estimate of EUR 140 million fell conveniently just below the EUR 150 million parliamentary approval threshold under the Law on State Property. From a rule-of-law and anti-corruption standpoint, the entire concession process requires independent judicial or prosecutorial scrutiny before proceeding further, not merely a new price tag on a flawed procedure.
assessments.executive_summary: The airport concession process poses serious rule-of-law concerns: three Tender Commission members resigned citing irregularities and undue influence, the IFC threatened withdrawal over transparency failures, and the valuation arrives suspiciously late given the EUR 150 million parliamentary threshold. The Ministry of Justice recommends an independent prosecutorial review of the entire tender process before any concession agreement advances to Parliament.
assessments.reasoning:
The Ministry of Justice identifies multiple serious legal and rule-of-law deficiencies in the airport concession process as it currently stands.

TENDER PROCESS INTEGRITY: The most alarming aspect is the well-documented collapse of tender integrity during 2025. Three members of the Tender Commission -- including the Prime Minister's advisor and two Ministry of Finance representatives -- resigned publicly, citing "possible irregularities, undue influence on certain commission members, and unequal treatment of bidders." This is not a minor procedural hiccup; it is an institutional alarm signal. The IFC, the World Bank's private sector arm and the government's own appointed consultant, threatened to withdraw from the process entirely if the Tender Commission did not provide adequate explanations for its scoring decisions. When the institution your government hired to guarantee process credibility considers walking away, the rule of law is in jeopardy. These events were widely reported by CdM, EX-YU Aviation News, and SeeNews, among others.

THE EUR 150 MILLION THRESHOLD AND VALUATION TIMING: Under Montenegro's Law on State Property, concession decisions for assets valued above EUR 150 million require parliamentary approval. The 2018 valuation placed Airports of Montenegro at EUR 140 million -- just EUR 10 million below this critical threshold. The suspiciously convenient proximity to the threshold raises legitimate questions about whether the original valuation was designed to keep the decision within executive authority and avoid parliamentary scrutiny. The fact that a new valuation is being commissioned only now, after the tender has been running since December 2024 and bids are due by May 9, 2026, suggests that the valuation is being treated as a procedural afterthought rather than a foundational prerequisite. A proper rule-of-law approach would have required the valuation to precede the tender launch, not follow it.

CONSTITUTIONAL AND PARLIAMENTARY OVERSIGHT: If the new valuation exceeds EUR 150 million -- which is likely given inflation, airport traffic growth, and infrastructure investments since 2018 -- the deal must go before Parliament. This is a critical constitutional safeguard for the disposal of major state assets. However, the parliamentary review will be meaningful only if Parliament receives full transparency about the tender irregularities, the IFC's concerns, and the reasons for Tender Commission resignations. The Ministry of Justice is concerned that rushing the valuation to meet the bidding timeline could compromise the thoroughness and independence of the assessment. The Airports of Montenegro union's demand for multiple independent appraisers, transparent methodology, and comparative analysis of concession versus state-managed development is legally sound and should be heeded.

EU ACCESSION IMPLICATIONS (CHAPTER 23): Montenegro adopted action plans for final benchmarks in Chapters 23 and 24 with a Q3 2026 implementation deadline. Chapter 23 specifically requires demonstrable progress on judicial independence, anti-corruption credibility, and rule of law. The European Commission's 2025 report noted that structural changes -- not merely administrative compliance -- are needed, including credible prosecution of high-level corruption and politically neutral institutions. A concession process marred by allegations of undue influence, commission resignations, and IFC withdrawal threats directly undermines Montenegro's Chapter 23 narrative. If the government proceeds without addressing these integrity concerns, the EU will have concrete evidence that anti-corruption commitments remain superficial.

ANTI-CORRUPTION FRAMEWORK: The allegations raised by departing Tender Commission members -- specifically regarding "undue influence" on evaluators -- fall squarely within the jurisdiction of the Agency for Prevention of Corruption (ASK) and potentially the Special State Prosecutor's Office (SDT). To the Ministry's knowledge, no formal investigation has been initiated into these allegations. This is a significant gap. Under Montenegro's anti-corruption legislation, credible allegations of influence over public procurement and concession decisions should trigger mandatory review. The Ministry of Justice notes that the Ombudsman for Property Rights (Bojana Cirovic) has already intervened by recommending a proper valuation, which suggests that independent oversight bodies recognize the process deficiencies.

LABOUR RIGHTS AND SOCIAL PROTECTIONS: The airport workers' union has raised legitimate concerns about employee protections under a potential 25-30 year concession. The concession agreement must include enforceable provisions on job security, working conditions, and collective bargaining rights consistent with EU social acquis and Montenegrin labor law. These are not merely economic considerations but legal obligations under the European Social Charter and the EU acquis Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment).

COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS: EU best practice for airport concessions of this magnitude -- as seen in Greece (regional airports concession to Fraport), Serbia (Belgrade Airport to Vinci), and Albania (Tirana to a Turkish consortium) -- typically involves comprehensive ex-ante valuation, parliamentary or equivalent legislative approval, robust anti-corruption safeguards, and post-award independent auditing. Montenegro's process falls short on most of these benchmarks. The Serbian Belgrade Airport concession, while not without criticism, at least involved a clear legislative framework and parliamentary debate prior to tender launch, not after.
key_concerns: Tender Commission integrity collapse: Three members resigned citing irregularities, undue influence, and unequal treatment of bidders, with no known prosecutorial investigation initiated into these serious allegations

Suspicious valuation timing: The 2018 valuation of EUR 140 million fell conveniently below the EUR 150 million parliamentary approval threshold, and the new valuation is being commissioned after the tender launched rather than before it

IFC withdrawal threat: The World Bank's IFC, hired as the government's own process consultant, threatened to leave the process over transparency and fairness failures -- a severe credibility blow to the entire procedure
Insufficient anti-corruption response: Despite credible allegations of undue influence on the Tender Commission, neither ASK nor the Special Prosecutor appear to have been engaged to investigate

EU Chapter 23 risk: Proceeding with a concession process tainted by integrity concerns directly contradicts Montenegro's commitments under Chapter 23 final benchmarks, with a Q3 2026 implementation deadline

Parliamentary oversight may be circumvented: If the valuation is conducted under time pressure to meet bidding deadlines, there is a risk it will understate asset value to avoid triggering the parliamentary approval requirement
recommendations: Mandate an independent prosecutorial review by the Special State Prosecutor (SDT) into the allegations of undue influence on Tender Commission members before the concession process advances further

Require the new valuation to be conducted by at least two independent international appraisers, with full public disclosure of methodology, assumptions, and results, as demanded by the airport workers' union

Ensure that Parliament receives a comprehensive dossier including all Tender Commission documentation, IFC correspondence, reasons for member resignations, and CAAP's formal complaints before any vote
Engage the Agency for Prevention of Corruption (ASK) to conduct a conflict-of-interest review of all remaining Tender Commission members and government officials involved in the concession process
Suspend the current bidding timeline if necessary to allow the valuation and integrity review to be completed without time pressure, even if this means extending the May 9 deadline
Include enforceable labor protection clauses in any concession agreement, covering job security, collective bargaining, and working conditions consistent with EU social acquis requirements

Commission a comparative cost-benefit analysis of concession versus continued state management with targeted private investment, as the union has requested, to ensure Parliament can make a fully informed decision

counter_proposal.title: Integrity-First Concession Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Justice would pause the current timeline, mandate prosecutorial and anti-corruption review of the tender irregularities, require dual independent international valuations with full public transparency, and ensure that Parliament receives complete information -- including the history of process failures -- before any vote. Only after these integrity safeguards are satisfied would the concession proceed, with strengthened legal protections for workers and municipalities built into the agreement.
key_changes: Suspend the May 9 bidding deadline until the integrity review is complete
Commission SDT investigation into Tender Commission resignation allegations
Require dual independent international valuations with published methodology
Mandate full parliamentary disclosure package including IFC correspondence and CAAP complaints
ASK conflict-of-interest screening for all remaining process participants
Build enforceable labor, municipal revenue-sharing, and service-quality clauses into the concession agreement
Establish an independent post-award monitoring body with civil society representation
expected_benefits: Restores credibility of the concession process domestically and internationally
Demonstrates genuine anti-corruption commitment aligned with EU Chapter 23 final benchmarks
Ensures parliamentary decision is based on complete and accurate information
Protects against future legal challenges and potential international arbitration
Safeguards workers' rights and municipal interests in a multi-decade agreement
Reduces risk of the concession being unwound by a future government due to procedural illegitimacy
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. Suspending the timeline will face resistance from the executive branch concerned about investor confidence and deal momentum. However, the legal basis for requiring integrity review is strong -- the Tender Commission resignations and IFC concerns are matters of public record. The SDT and ASK have existing mandates to investigate such matters. The main political obstacle is the government's desire to finalize the deal quickly, but proceeding without addressing documented irregularities creates far greater legal and reputational risk in the medium term, including potential international arbitration claims from aggrieved bidders.
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo pravde bi pauziralo trenutnu vremensku liniju, naložilo tužilačku i antikorupcijsku provjeru nepravilnosti u tenderu, zahtijevalo dvije nezavisne međunarodne procjene vrijednosti uz potpunu javnu transparentnost, te osiguralo da Parlament dobije potpune informacije -- uključujući istoriju procesnih propusta -- prije bilo kakvog glasanja. Tek nakon što budu ispunjene ove zaštitne mjere integriteta, koncesija bi se nastavila, uz ojačane pravne zaštite za radnike i opštine ugrađene u sporazum.
key_changes_mne: Suspendovati rok za podnošenje ponuda od 9. maja dok se ne završi provjera integriteta
Pokrenuti istragu SDT-a o navodima vezanim za ostavke članova Tenderske komisije
Zahtijevati dvije nezavisne međunarodne procjene vrijednosti sa objavljenom metodologijom
Naložiti dostavljanje potpunog paketa informacija Parlamentu, uključujući prepisku sa IFC-om i pritužbe u okviru CAAP-a
Provjera sukoba interesa od strane ASK-a za sve preostale učesnike u postupku
Ugraditi izvršive klauzule o radu, raspodjeli prihoda opštinama i kvalitetu usluga u ugovor o koncesiji
Uspostaviti nezavisno tijelo za praćenje nakon dodjele, uz zastupljenost civilnog društva
expected_benefits_mne: Obnavlja kredibilitet procesa koncesije na domaćem i međunarodnom nivou
Demonstrira istinsku posvećenost borbi protiv korupcije, usklađenu sa završnim mjerilima EU Poglavlja 23
Osigurava da parlamentarna odluka bude zasnovana na potpunim i tačnim informacijama
Štiti od budućih pravnih osporavanja i potencijalnih međunarodnih arbitražnih postupaka
Čuva prava radnika i interese opština u okviru sporazuma koji traje više decenija
Smanjuje rizik da buduća vlada poništi koncesiju zbog procesne nelegitimnosti
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Suspendovanje vremenske linije nailaziće na otpor izvršne vlasti zabrinute zbog povjerenja investitora i dinamike realizacije dogovora. Međutim, pravni osnov za zahtijevanje provjere integriteta je snažan -- ostavke članova Tenderske komisije i zabrinutosti IFC-a su stvari od javnog značaja. SDT i ASK imaju postojeće nadležnosti za istraživanje takvih pitanja. Glavna politička prepreka je želja Vlade da brzo finalizuje dogovor, ali nastavljanje bez otklanjanja dokumentovanih nepravilnosti stvara daleko veći pravni i reputacijski rizik na srednji rok, uključujući potencijalne zahtjeve za međunarodnu arbitražu od strane oštećenih ponuđača.

assessments.summary_mne: Odluka o naručivanju nove procjene vrijednosti imovine Aerodroma Crne Gore predstavlja proceduralno neophodan korak, ali dolazi daleko prekasno u procesu koncesije koji je već ozbiljno kompromitovan dokumentovanim nepravilnostima, ostavkama članova Tenderske komisije, prijetnjama povlačenja IFC-a i vjerodostojnim optužbama o nedozvoljenom uticaju na procjenitelje. Procjena vrijednosti rizikuje da postane puka proceduralna formalnost, a ne stvarna zaštitna mjera – naročito imajući u vidu da je procjena iz 2018. godine od 140 miliona EUR zgodno pala ispod zakonskog praga od 150 miliona EUR koji zahtijeva parlamentarno odobrenje u skladu sa Zakonom o državnoj imovini. Sa stanovišta vladavine prava i borbe protiv korupcije, cjelokupni proces koncesije zahtijeva nezavisnu sudsku ili tužilačku provjeru prije nego što se nastavi dalje – a ne samo novu cijenu na ionako manjkavoj proceduri.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Proces koncesije aerodroma izaziva ozbiljne zabrinutosti u pogledu vladavine prava: tri člana Tenderske komisije podnijela su ostavke navodeći nepravilnosti i nedozvoljeni uticaj, IFC je zaprijetio povlačenjem zbog propusta u transparentnosti, a procjena vrijednosti stiže sumnjivo kasno s obzirom na parlamentarni prag od 150 miliona EUR. Ministarstvo pravde preporučuje nezavisnu tužilačku reviziju cjelokupnog tenderskog procesa prije nego što bilo koji ugovor o koncesiji bude upućen Parlamentu.
key_concerns_mne: Narušavanje integriteta Tenderske komisije: Tri člana su podnijela ostavke navodeći nepravilnosti, nedozvoljeni uticaj i nejednako postupanje prema ponuđačima, a nije pokrenuta nijedna poznata tužilačka istraga u vezi sa ovim ozbiljnim optužbama

Sumnjivo vrijeme procjene vrijednosti: Procjena iz 2018. godine od 140 miliona EUR zgodno je pala ispod zakonskog praga od 150 miliona EUR koji zahtijeva parlamentarno odobrenje, a nova procjena se naručuje nakon pokretanja tendera, a ne prije njega

Prijetnja povlačenjem IFC-a: IFC Svjetske banke, angažovan kao konsultant vlade za praćenje procesa, zaprijetio je napuštanjem procesa zbog propusta u transparentnosti i pravičnosti – što je ozbiljan udarac na kredibilitet cjelokupne procedure

Nedovoljan odgovor u oblasti borbe protiv korupcije: Uprkos vjerodostojnim optužbama o nedozvoljenom uticaju na Tendersku komisiju, čini se da ni ASK ni Specijalno državno tužilaštvo nisu angažovani radi sprovođenja istrage

Rizik po Poglavlje 23 EU: Nastavak procesa koncesije opterećenog problemima integriteta direktno je u suprotnosti sa obavezama Crne Gore u okviru završnih mjerila Poglavlja 23, uz rok implementacije u trećem kvartalu 2026. godine

Parlamentarni nadzor može biti zaobiđen: Ukoliko se procjena vrijednosti sprovede pod vremenskim pritiskom radi ispunjenja rokova za podnošenje ponuda, postoji rizik da vrijednost imovine bude potcijenjena kako bi se izbjeglo aktiviranje zahtjeva za parlamentarnim odobrenjem
